metadata.description: Comprehensive swine disease database enriched from Diseases of Swine 11th Edition (Zimmerman et al., 2019). Includes detailed clinical signs, transmission, diagnosis, treatment, control, and vaccine recommendations for 104 swine diseases.
source: Diseases of Swine, 11th Edition - Zimmerman, Karriker, Ramirez, Schwartz, Stevenson, Zhang (Wiley-Blackwell, 2019)
metadata.intended_use: FarmWell PigWell diagnostic and biosecurity module - English version
metadata.notes: Multilingual versions (Indonesian, Vietnamese) to be generated separately via DeepSeek translation

diseases.name: App (Actinobacillus pleuropneumoniae)
diseases.description: Pleuropneumonia caused by Actinobacillus pleuropneumoniae (App), one of the most important swine bacterial respiratory pathogens found worldwide. At least 16 serotypes exist (biotype I: 14 serotypes; biotype II: 2 serotypes), with serotypes 1, 5, 9, 11 most virulent. Virulent strains can induce rapidly fatal fibrinohemorrhagic and necrotizing pleuropneumonia in pigs of all ages. Survivors often develop lung sequestra. Bacteria reside mainly in tonsils; carrier pigs may show no clinical signs.
clinicalSigns: Peracute form: sudden death within 24-36 hours with no premonitory signs
Acute form: severe respiratory distress with rapid labored breathing, open-mouth breathing
High fever (41.5°C / 107°F) with marked depression and anorexia
Blood-tinged or bloody froth from nose and mouth
Blue discoloration of skin (cyanosis), especially ears and extremities
Profuse hemorrhagic nasal discharge in severe cases
Reluctance to move; pigs lie in sternal recumbency
Chronic/subacute form: intermittent coughing, reduced growth, decreased feed intake
Mortality rate can exceed 20% in acute outbreaks in naive herds
Morbidity can approach 100% in susceptible populations during acute outbreaks
transmission: Direct contact with infected pigs via nasal secretions and respiratory droplets
Aerosol transmission over short distances (typically < 2 km; not long-range)
Organism survives only a few days outside the pig under normal conditions
Introduced into naive herds via carrier pigs — main route of new herd infection
Stress factors (weaning, transport, overcrowding) and coinfections (PRRS, influenza) trigger clinical disease
Carrier animals harbor organism subclinically in tonsils for months to years
No significant role for fomites or indirect transmission under field conditions
diagnosis: Peracute/acute diagnosis: sudden death + nasal hemorrhage + bloody froth + respiratory distress
Post-mortem: fibrinohemorrhagic and necrotizing pleuropneumonia with fibrinous pleuritis
Characteristic lung lesions: bilateral consolidation in cranioventral and caudodorsal areas
Laboratory: bacterial isolation from lung/pleural fluid on blood agar with NAD (staphylococcal nurse streak)
PCR for species-specific identification; serotyping for vaccine selection
Serology (ELISA against ApxIV toxin detects all serotypes) for herd surveillance
Differentials: Pasteurella multocida, Mycoplasma hyopneumoniae, PRRS, Streptococcus suis
treatment: Immediate mass injection of entire at-risk group — do not wait for individual identification
Broad-spectrum antibiotics: ceftiofur, florfenicol, tulathromycin, enrofloxacin (based on susceptibility testing)
Injectable route essential since sick pigs stop eating and drinking
Penicillin G effective for susceptible strains; resistance emerging in some regions
Continue treatment 3–5 days; early intervention critical for survival
Supportive therapy: anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) to reduce fever and lung inflammation
Isolate severely ill pigs to reduce transmission
control: Vaccination with serotype-specific or cross-protective subunit/toxoid vaccines
Select App-negative replacement breeding stock and test prior to introduction
Strategic in-feed medication (tiamulin, tetracyclines) during high-risk transition periods
All-in/all-out production with thorough cleaning and disinfection between groups
In outbreaks: examine pigs at least 3 times daily; remove and treat sick animals promptly
Reduce stress: optimize ventilation, stocking density, and mixing of groups
Eradication possible via partial depopulation or test-and-remove strategies
vaccineRecommendations: Serotype-specific autogenous or commercial bacterins (e.g., Porcilis APP)
Cross-protective subunit vaccine based on Apx toxins and OMP
Vaccination schedule: 2 doses at 6 and 8–10 weeks of age in endemic herds

diseases.name: Campylobacter infection
diseases.description: Subclinical to mild enteric infection caused by Campylobacter coli (predominant in swine) and C. jejuni. These organisms are commensals of the swine intestinal tract and rarely cause overt disease in pigs. Primary significance is as a zoonotic pathogen — swine represent an important reservoir for human campylobacteriosis, particularly C. coli strains.
clinicalSigns: Usually subclinical in pigs; colonization without overt disease is common
Mild, self-limiting watery diarrhea in young piglets (< 4 weeks)
Occasional mucoid yellowish feces with no blood
Mild depression without fever in affected piglets
Generally good survival rate; mortality rare unless concurrent infections present
In humans (zoonotic concern): acute gastroenteritis with diarrhea, abdominal cramps, fever
transmission: Fecal-oral transmission via contaminated environment, water, or feed
Environmental contamination from subclinically infected animals
Colonizes the intestinal tract of most pigs without causing disease
Shed in high numbers in feces; environmentally persistent in moist conditions
Contaminated carcasses at slaughter — primary source for human infections
diagnosis: Usually diagnosed retrospectively based on fecal culture
Culture on selective media (Campylobacter blood agar with antibiotics) at 42°C microaerophilically
Dark-field or phase contrast microscopy: characteristic darting/corkscrew motility
PCR for species differentiation (C. coli vs C. jejuni)
Clinical significance often uncertain given high prevalence as commensal
treatment: Usually self-limiting; specific treatment rarely necessary in pigs
If required: erythromycin, tylosin, or lincomycin based on susceptibility
Supportive electrolyte therapy if dehydration present in young piglets
Antibiotics rarely justified given self-limiting nature and zoonotic resistance concerns
control: Good hygiene and sanitation practices throughout production
Thorough cleaning and disinfection between groups
Maintain clean water supply; reduce fecal contamination of feed and water
Pre-harvest management to reduce Campylobacter load for food safety
No specific vaccines available for swine

diseases.name: Clostridium difficile
diseases.description: Anaerobic, spore-forming gram-positive bacterium causing neonatal typhlocolitis in piglets within the first 1–7 days of life. Produces toxins TcdA and TcdB causing intestinal necrosis. C. difficile is also a significant human pathogen and pigs may serve as a zoonotic reservoir. Spores are extremely resistant to environmental conditions and disinfectants.
clinicalSigns: Pasty to watery yellow diarrhea in piglets 1–7 days old (first week of life)
Characteristic mesocolonic edema (gelatinous edema in the colon mesentery) at necropsy
Scrotal edema in male piglets is a pathognomonic sign
Abdominal distension and discomfort
Rapidly progressing dehydration and weakness
Mortality can be significant in affected litters (20–50% in severe outbreaks)
Pseudomembranous colitis lesions at necropsy: gray-white fibrinonecrotic membrane on colon
transmission: Ubiquitous environmental spores — primary source of infection in neonates
Spores highly resistant to many common disinfectants, including chlorine-based products
Sow feces as source of colonization for neonates
Disruption of normal intestinal microbiome allows C. difficile overgrowth
Antibiotic use in sows or piglets increases risk by altering gut flora
Spores persist in farrowing facilities for extended periods despite cleaning
diagnosis: Clinical signs: scrotal/mesocolonic edema in neonatal piglets is highly suggestive
Post-mortem: mesocolonic edema, pseudomembranous colitis, fibrinonecrotic colitis
Laboratory: ELISA or PCR detection of toxins A and B (TcdA, TcdB) in fresh feces
Anaerobic culture on selective cycloserine-cefoxitin-fructose agar (CCFA)
Differentiate from Clostridium perfringens type C, E. coli, and rotavirus
treatment: Metronidazole is treatment of choice (oral or parenteral)
Vancomycin as alternative where available
Oral rehydration therapy and supportive care for dehydration
Early treatment within first 24 hours is critical for survival
Probiotic supplementation may help restore normal gut flora after treatment
control: Thorough farrowing facility cleaning and disinfection between batches
Use sporicide-based disinfectants (vaporized hydrogen peroxide, bleach at high concentration)
Ensure adequate colostrum intake by all piglets within first hours of life
Avoid unnecessary antibiotic use, especially in late-gestation sows
Improve overall farrowing hygiene to reduce environmental spore load

diseases.name: Clostridium novyi
diseases.description: Anaerobic, spore-forming bacterium causing sudden death with gangrenous cellulitis (gas gangrene). Organism enters through wounds from routine procedures (castration, tail docking, injection sites) and produces potent alpha-toxin causing necrosis. Spores persist in soil and environment. C. novyi type B is most associated with swine disease. Relatively uncommon but rapidly fatal with little warning.
clinicalSigns: Sudden death, often found dead without prior observation of illness
Rapidly expanding subcutaneous emphysema (gas under skin) at wound sites
Dark red to black discoloration of affected skin with crepitation on palpation
Severe soft tissue swelling at injection, castration, or tail docking sites
Depression and sudden collapse in peracute cases
Post-mortem: gangrenous necrosis, gas pockets in tissues, serosanguineous discharge
transmission: Spores ubiquitous in soil, feces, and environment
Entry through wounds: castration sites, tail docking, injection sites, bite wounds, fighting injuries
Anaerobic conditions in damaged tissue trigger spore germination and toxin production
Not transmitted animal-to-animal; sporadic occurrence linked to wound contamination
diagnosis: Often diagnosed at post-mortem: characteristic gangrenous lesions, gas in tissues, dark tissue discoloration
Fluorescent antibody test (FAT) on frozen tissue sections — rapid and specific
Anaerobic culture on blood agar: large colonies with double zone of hemolysis
PCR for genus/species identification
Post-mortem: gas pockets in subcutis and muscle, gelatinous edema, dark necrotic tissue
treatment: Usually too rapid for effective treatment
High-dose penicillin G if detected in early stages
Surgical debridement of necrotic tissue if animal survives initial insult
Supportive care: fluids, NSAIDs
Prevention is far more effective than treatment
control: Minimize wound contamination during routine procedures
Use clean, sharp instruments for castration and tail docking
Disinfect instruments and injection sites
Perinatal processing hygiene: clean farrowing areas, sterile equipment
Vaccination with Clostridial bacterin-toxoid where disease is endemic
vaccineRecommendations: Clostridial bacterin-toxoid (multi-valent) where endemic disease is documented

diseases.name: Clostridium perfringens
diseases.description: Clostridium perfringens type C causes acute necrohemorrhagic enteritis in piglets during the first 1–3 days of life, with high mortality. Type A causes a milder enteritis in older piglets and weaners. Both types produce toxins (beta-toxin for type C; alpha-toxin for type A) causing intestinal necrosis. Type C is a major cause of neonatal mortality in piglets worldwide.
clinicalSigns: Type C (neonates 1-3 days): bloody or hemorrhagic diarrhea — reddish-brown liquid feces
Rapid progression from bloody diarrhea to death within 12–24 hours
Peracute type C: sudden death in piglets 1–3 days old without diarrhea
Subacute type C: pasty pale feces, poor weight gain, death within 5–7 days
Type A (1-4 weeks): creamy-grey non-hemorrhagic pasty diarrhea
Dehydration, weakness, and inability to nurse in affected piglets
Severely affected piglets exhibit abdominal distension and crying
Mortality in untreated type C cases: 50–100% of affected litter
transmission: Spores ubiquitous in soil, dust, and feces
Sow feces and environment are primary sources for neonatal exposure
Type C: ingestion of large numbers of bacteria causes rapid beta-toxin production
Type A: overgrowth favored by high-carbohydrate diet, stress, or dysbiosis
Diet change, weaning stress, and antibiotic disruption of flora predispose
diagnosis: Type C: clinical signs (bloody diarrhea in 1-3 day old piglets) highly suggestive
Post-mortem: dark red to black necrohemorrhagic jejunum/ileum with gas in mucosa
Type A: jejunal contents creamy, foamy; microscopic changes milder than type C
Laboratory: Gram stain showing large gram-positive rods; anaerobic culture
ELISA or PCR for toxin type identification (beta-toxin gene for type C, alpha-toxin for type A)
treatment: Early type C: oral antitoxin administration (anti-beta toxin serum) — most effective if given within first hours
Antibiotics: penicillin G, amoxicillin, ampicillin — limited efficacy in advanced cases
Supportive care: oral fluids and electrolytes for type A
Treatment often unsatisfactory in acute type C; prevention more effective
control: Vaccination of sows: 2 doses before farrowing with C. perfringens type C/D toxoid
Maternal immunity via colostrum protects neonates — most effective prevention strategy
Ensure all piglets receive adequate colostrum within first 6 hours of birth
Improve farrowing hygiene: clean and disinfect farrowing crates
Prophylactic penicillin to entire litter at birth in problem herds
vaccineRecommendations: C. perfringens type C/D toxoid — 2 doses to sows pre-farrowing (primary vaccination)
Annual booster 3–4 weeks before farrowing in previously vaccinated sows

diseases.name: Mycoplasma suis (Eperythrozoonosis)
diseases.description: Mycoplasma suis (formerly Eperythrozoon suis) is a hemotropic mycoplasma that attaches to and destroys red blood cells causing hemolytic anemia. Primarily affects splenectomized or immunocompromised pigs. In intact pigs, infection is usually latent; clinical disease can be triggered by stress, concurrent disease, or immunosuppression. Transmitted by blood-feeding insects and contaminated needles.
clinicalSigns: Acute eperythrozoonosis: sudden pallor, icterus (jaundice), fever (41-42°C)
Skin discoloration: pale to icteric (yellow-orange) or reddish-blue
Weakness, exercise intolerance, staggering gait
Respiratory distress due to severe anemia
Posterior weakness or paralysis in severe cases
Reproductive failure in sows: increased returns to service, small litters, weak piglets
Wasting and poor growth performance in growing pigs
High mortality in splenectomized pigs or those with severe immunosuppression
transmission: Blood-sucking insects: lice (Haematopinus suis), mosquitoes, flies
Contaminated needles during vaccination, treatment, or ear tagging
Transplacental transmission in acutely infected sows
Blood transfusion or blood-contaminated equipment
Carrier pigs are the primary reservoir; stress triggers recrudescence
diagnosis: Blood smear: Giemsa or acridine orange staining reveals organisms on erythrocyte surface
PCR most sensitive and specific test for detection
CBC: regenerative hemolytic anemia, low PCV (packed cell volume)
Serology: ELISA, indirect immunofluorescence antibody (IIFA) for antibody detection
Differential diagnosis: iron deficiency anemia, hemorrhage, other hemolytic diseases
treatment: Doxycycline or oxytetracycline (10-20 mg/kg/day) for 5-10 days — treatment of choice
Tiamulin in combination with tetracyclines for enhanced efficacy
Blood transfusion for severely anemic animals
Iron supplementation for piglets
Supportive care: corticosteroids cautiously to suppress hemolytic process
control: Control blood-sucking insects: lice treatment with acaricides (ivermectin, amitraz)
Use sterile single-use needles for all injections and blood collection
Reduce stress factors that can trigger recrudescence
Isolate and treat affected animals to prevent needle-borne transmission
No commercial vaccines available

diseases.name: Actinobacillosis
diseases.description: Actinobacillus suis causes septicemia, pleuropneumonia, polyarthritis, and skin hemorrhages primarily in young pigs and suckling piglets. Clinical presentation resembles App pleuropneumonia but has different epidemiological implications. A. suis is also a commensal of the upper respiratory tract in healthy pigs. Associated with sudden deaths in apparently healthy animals. Other Actinobacillus species (A. equuli) occasionally cause septicemia.
clinicalSigns: Sudden death in young pigs without premonitory signs
Acute septicemia: high fever, depression, anorexia, reluctance to move
Petechial and ecchymotic hemorrhages on skin (especially ears, abdomen)
Purplish-red discoloration of skin in acute septicemia
Respiratory distress: rapid breathing, labored breathing
Arthritis: joint swelling and lameness
Polyserositis: fibrinous pericarditis, pleuritis, peritonitis
Skin necrosis in chronic cases
High mortality in neonates and young suckling piglets
transmission: Commensal of upper respiratory tract in healthy carrier pigs
Opportunistic pathogen under conditions of stress or immunosuppression
Direct contact and respiratory droplets between pigs
Transmission from sow to offspring during farrowing
Stress factors: weaning, mixing, transport trigger clinical disease
diagnosis: Lesions at necropsy: hemorrhages, fibrinous polyserositis, pleuropneumonia
Bacterial culture from blood, lung, joints — grows on MacConkey and blood agar (unlike App biotype I, grows without NAD)
PCR for species differentiation from A. pleuropneumoniae
Biochemical tests: urease positive, NAD-independent
Serological differentiation from App important for control planning
treatment: Broad-spectrum antibiotics: penicillin G, ampicillin, trimethoprim-sulfa
Ceftiofur, enrofloxacin for severe cases
Early aggressive parenteral treatment for best results
Often higher antibiotic resistance than A. pleuropneumoniae
control: Strict farrowing hygiene: clean, disinfected farrowing facilities
Ensure adequate colostrum intake for all neonates
Reduce stress during transition periods
Autogenous bacterin vaccination in problem herds
Antimicrobial prophylaxis at farrowing in endemic herds
vaccineRecommendations: Autogenous bacterin in endemic herds

diseases.name: Anthrax
diseases.description: Bacillus anthracis causes anthrax, a rare but potentially fatal disease of pigs characterized by sudden death, internal hemorrhages, and septicemia. Pigs are relatively resistant compared to ruminants but can be infected through ingestion of contaminated feed (bone meal, blood meal). Anthrax is a notifiable disease in most countries and a major zoonotic concern. Spores can persist in soil for decades.
clinicalSigns: Sudden death in pigs with minimal or no premonitory signs
Pharyngeal/cervical form (most common in pigs): acute throat swelling with pharyngeal edema
Difficulty swallowing, breathing, and opening mouth
High fever, depression, anorexia in surviving animals
Bloody discharge from orifices (septicemia form)
Bloody, unclotted blood at necropsy — characteristic finding
Swollen, dark red to black spleen (splenomegaly)
Bloody effusions in body cavities
transmission: Ingestion of contaminated bone meal, blood meal, or other animal-derived feed products
Soil contaminated with anthrax spores — spores persist for decades
Rare aerosol exposure in contaminated environments
NO direct pig-to-pig transmission
ZOONOTIC: Highly dangerous to humans — report immediately to authorities
diagnosis: NOTIFIABLE DISEASE: Immediately notify veterinary authorities if suspected
DO NOT perform post-mortem examination — risk of sporulation and environmental contamination
Peripheral blood smear: polychrome methylene blue (M'Fadyean) stain reveals large encapsulated rods
Bacteriological culture under biosafety conditions at reference laboratory
Ascoli ring precipitation test on tissue extracts
PCR on blood or tissue samples
treatment: Penicillin G: drug of choice if detected early (5,000–10,000 IU/kg every 12 hours)
Oxytetracycline or ciprofloxacin as alternatives
Treatment rarely successful in acute cases — early detection critical
Strict quarantine and biosecurity measures mandatory
control: NOTIFIABLE: Immediate notification of veterinary authorities required
Vaccination with live spore vaccine (Sterne strain) in endemic areas
Do not open carcasses — burn or bury with quicklime in situ
Quarantine affected premises for minimum 21 days
Avoid feeding bone meal/blood meal from unknown sources
Post-exposure prophylaxis for humans: penicillin or ciprofloxacin
vaccineRecommendations: Anthrax live spore vaccine (Sterne strain) in endemic regions — annual vaccination

diseases.name: Atrophic Rhinitis
diseases.description: Atrophic rhinitis (AR) is caused by toxigenic Pasteurella multocida type D producing dermonecrotic toxin (DNT), often with Bordetella bronchiseptica as a predisposing agent. Results in progressive destruction and atrophy of the nasal turbinate bones, leading to lateral deviation of the snout, sneezing, and impaired growth. Non-progressive form caused by B. bronchiseptica alone is generally self-limiting.
clinicalSigns: Sneezing and nasal discharge (serous to mucopurulent) in young piglets 2–8 weeks
Epistaxis (nosebleed) with blood-stained discharge in some cases
Progressive turbinate atrophy leads to shortened, wrinkled snout
Lateral deviation (twisting) of snout — hallmark lesion of progressive AR
Cephalic deformity — shortening of upper jaw, upturned snout
Lacrimal discharge (tear staining) — dark staining below eyes
Reduced feed intake and reduced daily weight gain
In severe progressive AR: pneumonia may develop as secondary complication
transmission: Bordetella bronchiseptica: primary colonizer of nasal mucosa in young piglets; spread by aerosol
Toxigenic Pasteurella multocida type D: primary cause of progressive atrophy
Sow-to-piglet transmission in first days of life — key window of infection
Aerosol transmission within herd; introduced via carrier pigs or gilts
Environmental factors: poor ventilation, high ammonia levels, dusty conditions predispose
diagnosis: Clinical examination: snout deviation, facial shortening, nasal discharge
Post-mortem turbinate scoring: nasal cross-section at level of first premolar tooth
Scoring system: 0 (normal) to 5 (complete atrophy) — score ≥ 2 is significant
Bacteriology: nasal swabs cultured for Bordetella and P. multocida
Toxin detection: PCR for DNT gene in P. multocida isolates
Serology: ELISA for anti-DNT antibodies in herd monitoring
treatment: In-feed/water tetracyclines or sulfonamides during high-risk periods (1–8 weeks of age)
Treatment of clinical cases: trimethoprim-sulfa, amoxicillin, ceftiofur
Parenteral treatment for individual animals with severe epistaxis
Turbinate damage is irreversible — early intervention most effective
control: Vaccination of sows: inactivated toxigenic P. multocida / B. bronchiseptica vaccines
Colostral immunity from vaccinated sows protects piglets in first weeks
Select Bordetella/P. multocida toxigenic-negative replacement gilts
Improve ventilation, reduce ammonia and dust levels
All-in/all-out production with thorough cleaning and disinfection
vaccineRecommendations: Inactivated P. multocida toxoid + B. bronchiseptica bacterin
Sow vaccination: 2 doses pre-farrowing, annual boosters

diseases.name: Bordetellosis (Bordetella bronchiseptica)
diseases.description: Bordetella bronchiseptica causes non-progressive atrophic rhinitis (NPAR) and bronchopneumonia in pigs. It is an important predisposing pathogen that damages nasal mucosa, facilitating colonization by toxigenic Pasteurella multocida (progressive AR). B. bronchiseptica is ubiquitous in swine populations and colonizes nasal passages without causing disease in many cases. Stress and poor ventilation promote clinical disease.
clinicalSigns: Sneezing and serous to mucopurulent nasal discharge in piglets 2–6 weeks
Mild to moderate non-progressive turbinate atrophy (self-limiting)
Mild respiratory signs: coughing, mild dyspnea
Reduced growth rate and feed conversion
Severe pneumonia in susceptible young piglets exposed to high challenge doses
Signs resolve when pigs develop immunity (usually by 8–12 weeks)
transmission: Aerosol and direct contact transmission
Sow-to-piglet transmission in early life — primary route
Widespread in swine populations; most herds are positive
Survives in environment for limited time; direct animal contact most important
diagnosis: Nasal swabs with culture on selective media (MacConkey or blood agar)
Characteristic wrinkled colony morphology; hemolysis on blood agar
PCR for B. bronchiseptica
Turbinate scoring at slaughter or post-mortem (non-progressive NPAR: mild, bilateral, symmetric)
treatment: In-feed tetracyclines or trimethoprim-sulfa during high-risk period (0–8 weeks)
Individual treatment: amoxicillin, trimethoprim-sulfa for clinical cases
Improvement in environmental conditions often as effective as antibiotic treatment
control: Vaccination: combined B. bronchiseptica / P. multocida toxoid vaccines for sows
Improve ventilation and reduce ammonia concentrations
All-in/all-out farrowing with thorough disinfection
Source negative gilts where possible
vaccineRecommendations: Inactivated B. bronchiseptica bacterin (alone or combined with P. multocida toxoid)
Sow vaccination pre-farrowing; piglet vaccination at 1–3 weeks in endemic herds

diseases.name: Botulism
diseases.description: Clostridium botulinum produces botulinum neurotoxin (BoNT), the most potent toxin known, causing progressive flaccid paralysis. Pigs ingest pre-formed toxin from contaminated feed (silage, rotting carcasses, soil). Type C botulism most common in swine. Rare in pigs compared to other species but can cause farm outbreaks when contaminated feed sources are implicated.
clinicalSigns: Progressive ascending flaccid paralysis beginning in hindquarters
Weakness leading to inability to stand (pigs found in lateral recumbency)
Dysphagia (difficulty swallowing) — tongue and jaw weakness
Mydriasis (dilated pupils) with loss of pupillary light reflex
Reduced or absent anal tone
Constipation and reduced intestinal motility
Death from respiratory paralysis
Normal mental status despite severe motor dysfunction (conscious but paralyzed)
transmission: Ingestion of pre-formed toxin in contaminated feed: spoiled silage, fermented materials, carcasses
Wound botulism (rare): C. botulinum spore germination and toxin production in wound
Toxicoinfectious botulism in very young animals: spore ingestion and gut germination
No pig-to-pig transmission
Improperly stored wet feed, silage, or decomposing organic material are high-risk sources
diagnosis: Clinical diagnosis: progressive flaccid paralysis + history of potentially contaminated feed
Laboratory: mouse bioassay (gold standard) — detection of botulinum toxin in serum/stomach contents
ELISA for botulinum toxin types in feed and biological samples
PCR for BoNT genes in suspect feed or feces
Necropsy: no characteristic gross lesions — diagnosis requires toxin detection
treatment: Remove and destroy contaminated feed source immediately
Trivalent antitoxin (types A, B, E or C, D, E depending on region) — expensive, limited availability
Supportive care: assisted feeding, fluid therapy, comfortable bedding
Neostigmine to partially counteract neuromuscular block
Nursing care: prevent decubitus ulcers, aspiration pneumonia
Prognosis poor in severe cases
control: Proper silage management: adequate acidification (pH < 4.5), avoid soil contamination
Remove carcasses promptly from environment — prevent decomposition near feed
Proper storage and handling of wet feeds and fermented products
Vaccination available for types C and D (combined toxoid) in some countries

diseases.name: Brucellosis
diseases.description: Brucella suis causes brucellosis in swine, a zoonotic disease characterized by abortion, infertility, orchitis, spondylitis, and lameness. B. suis has 5 biovars; biovar 1, 2, and 3 primarily infect pigs. Brucellosis is notifiable in most countries. Infected pigs may shed bacteria for years without clinical signs. Wild boar serve as a reservoir. Poses serious public health risk to farmers, veterinarians, and slaughterhouse workers.
clinicalSigns: Abortion: typically at 30–90 days of gestation; may occur any time during pregnancy
Return to estrus at irregular intervals following reproductive failure
Stillbirths and weak or mummified piglets in infected litters
Orchitis in boars: unilateral or bilateral testicular swelling, hardening, pain
Epididymitis and reduced semen quality; sterility in severe cases
Spondylitis: posterior paralysis or paresis due to vertebral abscesses (chronic form)
Arthritis: joint swelling, lameness particularly in hind limbs
Generally normal appearance between episodes of reproductive failure
transmission: Venereal transmission: most important route — semen from infected boars
Ingestion of infected uterine discharge, placentas, aborted fetuses, or milk
Direct contact with reproductive secretions during mating or parturition
Oral route: sniffing or licking infected birth products
Wild boar as reservoir — contact at fence lines or shared grazing
ZOONOTIC: Humans infected by direct contact with infected animals/tissues or unpasteurized milk
diagnosis: NOTIFIABLE DISEASE in most countries
Serology: Rose Bengal Plate Test (RBPT) as screening; SAT, CFT, and ELISA for confirmation
Bacteriological culture from blood, semen, placenta, lymph nodes (requires BSL-2 laboratory)
PCR for Brucella DNA in tissues, semen, or blood
Abortion differentials: PRRS, PPV, leptospirosis, CSF, pseudorabies
treatment: NO treatment is recommended; infected animals should be culled
Antibiotic treatment (tetracyclines) may suppress signs but does not eliminate infection
Depopulation and premises disinfection are the primary control measures
control: NOTIFIABLE: Report to authorities; stamping-out policy in most countries
Test all replacement stock; purchase only from Brucellosis-free herds
Boars used for AI: regular serological testing
Prevent contact with wild boar: secure fencing
Biosecurity at slaughter: personal protective equipment for workers
No commercial vaccines licensed for swine in most countries

diseases.name: Colibacillosis (E. coli diarrhea)
diseases.description: Colibacillosis caused by enterotoxigenic Escherichia coli (ETEC) is one of the most economically important diseases of swine, causing neonatal diarrhea, post-weaning diarrhea (PWD), and edema disease. Different ETEC strains express different fimbriae (F4, F5, F6, F41 for neonates; F4, F18 for post-weaning) and toxins (STa, STb, LT heat-labile). Edema disease is caused by Shiga toxin 2e (Stx2e) producing E. coli.
clinicalSigns: Neonatal diarrhea (0–7 days): profuse watery to creamy white/yellow diarrhea
Rapid dehydration, sunken eyes, wrinkled skin, cold extremities in neonates
High mortality in untreated neonatal colibacillosis outbreaks
Post-weaning diarrhea (PWD, 7–14 days post-weaning): watery to pasty diarrhea, yellow-grey
Anorexia, poor growth, weight loss in weaners
Edema disease: sudden death in best-growing pigs at 4–8 weeks; eyelid edema, submandibular edema
Neurological signs in edema disease: ataxia, recumbency, head pressing, circling
Coliform mastitis in sows: swollen, painful mammary glands, reduced milk production
transmission: Fecal-oral transmission via contaminated environment, water, sow feces
Ingestion of ETEC from environment — primary route for neonatal infection
Inadequate colostrum intake increases susceptibility in neonates
Weaning stress, diet changes, and commingling promote post-weaning E. coli outbreaks
High bacterial load in contaminated farrowing and nursery environments
diagnosis: Clinical signs + herd history often sufficient for presumptive diagnosis
Fecal culture: E. coli hemolytic colonies on blood agar
PCR or ELISA for specific virulence factors: fimbriae (F4/F18), toxin genes (STa, STb, LT, Stx2e)
Post-mortem: watery intestinal contents, small intestinal distension, minimal gross lesions
Edema disease: post-mortem reveals gelatinous edema of gastric submucosa, mesocolon, brain stem
treatment: Oral electrolyte therapy (ORT) is cornerstone of neonatal treatment
Antibiotics based on sensitivity: trimethoprim-sulfa, neomycin, colistin, apramycin
Zinc oxide (2500 ppm) in starter feed highly effective for PWD control (regulatory restrictions in EU)
Organic acids in water/feed: reduce enteric pathogen load
Edema disease: no effective treatment; anti-inflammatory drugs may reduce brain edema
control: Ensure adequate colostrum intake: at least 200 ml in first 24 hours for neonates
Vaccination of sows: E. coli fimbrial vaccines (F4, F5, F6, F41) to boost colostral immunity
Optimize weaning age, diet, and environment to reduce PWD risk
Farrowing hygiene: clean, disinfected facilities; keep piglets warm and dry
Reduce stocking density in nursery; avoid mixing of litters at weaning
vaccineRecommendations: Sow vaccination with E. coli fimbrial vaccines (K88/F4, F5, F6, F41) pre-farrowing
K88 (F4) and F18 vaccines available for piglets in some countries

diseases.name: Colitis (Spirochetal colitis)
diseases.description: Spirochetal colitis caused by weakly hemolytic Brachyspira species (B. pilosicoli, B. murdochii, B. intermedia) causing porcine intestinal spirochetosis (PIS) and mild colitis. Distinguished from swine dysentery (B. hyodysenteriae) by milder clinical signs and lesions. B. pilosicoli causes loose mucoid feces and reduced growth in growing-finishing pigs. Often endemic in herds with poor hygiene.
clinicalSigns: Loose, mucoid, dark green-brown or grey feces (not bloody)
Reduced growth rate and poor feed conversion in affected pigs
Mild to moderate diarrhea — less severe than swine dysentery
Intermittent diarrhea with variable severity within groups
No fever typically; mildly reduced feed intake
Low mortality; high morbidity in affected grow-finish pigs
transmission: Fecal-oral transmission: contaminated feed, water, fomites
Carrier pigs and rodents as reservoirs
Introduced via purchased carrier pigs
Environment contamination: survives in moist conditions for days to weeks
diagnosis: Clinical signs: mucoid loose feces in grow-finish pigs, no blood, low mortality
Fecal culture on selective blood agar under microaerophilic conditions at 42°C
Dark-field microscopy: weakly hemolytic spirochetes (fewer coils than B. hyodysenteriae)
PCR for species identification
Post-mortem: mild typhlocolitis, mucus hypersecretion in spiral colon
treatment: Tiamulin, lincomycin, or pleuromutilins (valnemulin) in feed or water
Tylosin, tetracyclines as alternatives
Treatment duration 10–14 days for clinical resolution
control: Strict all-in/all-out with thorough cleaning and disinfection
Rodent control — important reservoir
Prevent introduction via testing of replacement stock
Improve hygiene: clean water supply, reduce fecal contamination of feed

diseases.name: Edema disease
diseases.description: Edema disease is caused by Shiga toxin 2e (Stx2e, formerly VT2e) producing E. coli strains (most commonly F18+ strains). Toxin damages vascular endothelium causing edema, primarily affecting the best-growing pigs within 1–2 weeks of weaning. Stx2e is absorbed from the gut and targets small vessels causing characteristic edema of the gastric submucosa, mesocolon, eyelids, and brain. High mortality in individual affected pigs.
clinicalSigns: Sudden death in the best-growing pigs in the pen (most common presentation)
Eyelid edema: periorbital swelling, swollen puffy eyelids
Submandibular edema (bottle jaw appearance)
Neurological signs: unsteady gait, ataxia, proprioceptive deficits
Convulsions, paddling movements, head pressing in severe neurological cases
Sudden onset with most pigs previously appearing healthy
Skin discoloration: cyanosis or erythema
Affected pigs 4–12 weeks old (post-weaning)
transmission: F18 E. coli colonizes intestinal tract at weaning; stress and diet change favor overgrowth
Fecal-oral transmission within groups
Stx2e absorbed from intestine — systemic distribution causing vascular damage
High-energy post-weaning diets associated with increased edema disease incidence
diagnosis: Clinical: sudden death in best-growing pigs + edema at necropsy
Post-mortem: gelatinous edema in gastric submucosa (pathognomonic), mesocolon, eyelids
Histopathology: angiopathy of small arteries and arterioles
Fecal/intestinal culture: F18 E. coli on selective agar
PCR: Stx2e gene in E. coli isolates or directly in intestinal contents
treatment: No effective specific treatment — supportive care only
Anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) to reduce brain edema
Reduce dietary intake temporarily: restrict feed access to reduce toxin production
Oral or parenteral electrolytes
control: Vaccination: F18 fimbrial vaccine (oral or parenteral) to prevent intestinal colonization
Zinc oxide (2500 ppm) in first 2 weeks post-weaning (effective where permitted)
Avoid high-energy diets at weaning; gradual transition to post-weaning diet
Reduce stress at weaning: maintain pen groupings, minimize mixing
Colistin in feed as metaphylaxis in high-risk groups (where regulatory allowed)
vaccineRecommendations: F18 fimbrial oral vaccine for piglets at weaning

diseases.name: Enzootic Pneumonia (EP) - Mycoplasma hyopneumoniae
diseases.description: Enzootic pneumonia (EP) caused by Mycoplasma hyopneumoniae is the most prevalent and economically important respiratory disease of swine worldwide. Infection causes chronic bronchopneumonia with characteristic cranioventral consolidation. M. hyopneumoniae impairs mucociliary clearance and predisposes to secondary bacterial infections (Pasteurella multocida, APP, Bordetella). Component of porcine respiratory disease complex (PRDC). Direct annual losses from reduced growth, feed costs, and treatment are enormous.
clinicalSigns: Dry, nonproductive hacking cough — hallmark sign; can persist weeks to months
Reduced growth rate and feed conversion in affected pigs
Low-grade fever (39.5–40°C) in early infection
Increased respiratory rate; mild to moderate dyspnea at exercise
Clinical signs most severe in growing-finishing pigs (8–22 weeks)
Secondary infections dramatically worsen clinical presentation
Morbidity can approach 100% in naive herds; mortality low with M. hyo alone
transmission: Direct contact — respiratory droplets and nasal secretions
Sow-to-piglet transmission in first weeks of life (vertical transmission critical)
Short-range airborne transmission within facilities
Long-range airborne transmission documented over distances of 3–10 km between farms
Persistent infection in lungs for several months after clinical recovery
Introduced via subclinically infected replacement gilts/boars
diagnosis: Clinical diagnosis: chronic dry cough in growing pigs with reduced growth
Post-mortem: bilateral cranioventral consolidation ('hepatized') lung lesions
Lung lesion scoring at slaughter: percentage of lung consolidation
PCR on bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) or lung tissue — most sensitive
ELISA serology for antibody detection in sow herds (positive from 4–6 weeks post-infection)
Fluorescent antibody test (FAT) on lung sections
Differentiate from PRRS, Pasteurellosis, App, and other respiratory diseases
treatment: Tiamulin + chlortetracycline combination: most effective
Fluoroquinolones (enrofloxacin, marbofloxacin) effective but cost-limiting
Lincomycin, tilmicosin, tulathromycin for individual or mass medication
Treat for minimum 10–14 days; relapse common with short treatment courses
Secondary bacterial infections (P. multocida) require appropriate additional antibiotics
control: Vaccination: commercially available modified live or inactivated vaccines (1 or 2 dose programs)
Vaccination at 3–6 weeks of age; reduces lung lesion score and improves growth
Eradication via SEW (segregated early weaning) + negative replacement stock
Medicated early weaning (MEW) with strategic antibiotic program
Reduce stocking density; improve ventilation to minimize aerosol transmission
Gilt acclimatization program before introduction into breeding herd
vaccineRecommendations: Mycoplasma hyopneumoniae inactivated bacterin (single or 2-dose regimen)
Modified live M. hyo vaccine (intranasal, single dose)
Vaccination at 3–8 weeks of age — reduces severity and lung lesions

diseases.name: Erysipelas (Erysipelothrix rhusiopathiae)
diseases.description: Erysipelas caused by Erysipelothrix rhusiopathiae is a major bacterial disease of swine worldwide. Presents in three forms: acute septicemia (sudden death, fever, skin lesions), subacute (diamond skin disease — pathognomonic rhomboid skin lesions), and chronic (vegetative endocarditis, arthritis). All ages affected; most common in 3–12 month old pigs. Zoonotic risk for handlers. Penicillin highly effective.
clinicalSigns: Acute septicemia: sudden death, high fever (42°C/107.6°F), bright red flushing of skin
Diamond skin disease (DSS): raised, firm, rhomboid-shaped urticaria — pathognomonic
Skin lesions: pink to purple irregular plaques on ears, snout, abdomen, back
Affected animals stand with arched back, reluctant to walk
Chronic arthritis: progressive joint swelling and lameness in one or more joints
Chronic vegetative endocarditis: exercise intolerance, sudden death, heart murmur
Pyrexia in acute form followed by rapid deterioration
Pale mucous membranes and weakness in septicemia
transmission: Organism widely distributed in environment: soil, feces, water, decaying organic matter
Carrier pigs: up to 50% of healthy pigs may carry organism in tonsils
Oral-fecal route: ingestion via contaminated feed or water
Skin abrasions and wounds provide entry points
Wild birds, fish, and other animals as environmental reservoirs
Environmental contamination persists for years in alkaline soils
diagnosis: Acute: sudden death + high fever + characteristic rhomboid skin lesions
Response to penicillin treatment within 24 hours supports clinical diagnosis
Bacterial culture: grows on blood agar as smooth gray colonies; gram-positive rod
CAMP test positive; H2S production on lead acetate paper
Serology: indirect fluorescent antibody, growth agglutination test
Chronic arthritis: firm, enlarged joints with pannus formation at necropsy
treatment: Penicillin G: drug of choice — highly effective within 12–24 hours
Amoxicillin, ampicillin as alternatives
Dose: 6.6–11 mg/kg penicillin G twice daily for 3–5 days
Early treatment critical — acute cases can die within 24–48 hours
Chronic arthritis: limited response to antibiotics once joint damage established
control: Vaccination: most effective control strategy
Live attenuated or inactivated vaccines available
Primary: 2 doses 3–4 weeks apart; boosters every 6 months for high-risk pigs
Sow vaccination pre-farrowing provides colostral protection to piglets
Maintain herd immunity through regular vaccination programs
Remove carcasses promptly; disinfect contaminated areas
vaccineRecommendations: Live attenuated E. rhusiopathiae vaccine (oral or injectable)
Inactivated bacterin
Booster every 6 months in finishing pigs; pre-farrowing in sows

diseases.name: Exudative Epidermitis (Greasy Pig Disease)
diseases.description: Greasy pig disease caused by Staphylococcus hyicus producing exfoliative toxins (ExhA, ExhB, ExhC, ExhD) that cleave desmoglein-1 in epidermal cells causing superficial exfoliative dermatitis. Primarily affects suckling and early weaned piglets (1–6 weeks). Disease spreads rapidly within litters. Severity ranges from mild localized lesions to generalized body involvement with high mortality. S. hyicus colonizes skin and enters via abrasions.
clinicalSigns: Sudden onset of greasy, oily appearance to skin — exudation of serum and sebaceous material
Brown-to-black crusty exudates covering entire body surface in severe cases
Lesions begin as small vesicles or erythematous areas on skin (face, periaortal area, legs)
Rapid spread to trunk and entire body within 24–48 hours in acute cases
Skin feels wet, greasy to touch; characteristic rancid/musty odor
Dehydration and weakness in severely affected animals
High fever in some acute cases
Mortality up to 80% in untreated young suckling piglets
transmission: S. hyicus is a normal skin commensal; disease triggered by skin trauma
Abrasions from teeth (fighting), rough flooring, trough edges provide entry
Direct contact between piglets within litter
Sow skin can be source of S. hyicus for neonates
Introduced into naive herds via carrier pigs or contaminated equipment
diagnosis: Clinical: characteristic greasy, exudative skin lesions in suckling piglets
Bacterial culture from fresh skin lesions or exudate: S. hyicus (coagulase positive/negative)
Toxin typing: PCR for exfoliative toxin genes (ExhA, ExhB, ExhC, ExhD)
Histopathology: superficial epidermolytic changes, subcorneal clefts
Differentials: mange, ringworm, pityriasis rosea, greasy pig disease vs dermatitis
treatment: Systemic antibiotics: penicillin, amoxicillin-clavulanate, trimethoprim-sulfa
Topical: antiseptic washes (chlorhexidine), zinc sulfate sprays
Supportive care: fluids, warmth, soft bedding
Clip needle teeth of piglets to prevent skin wounds from fighting
Early aggressive treatment critical — 3–5 days parenteral antibiotics
control: Clip needle teeth at birth using proper technique (minimize skin trauma)
Provide smooth, non-abrasive flooring in farrowing area
Autogenous bacterin vaccination in problem herds
Maximize colostrum intake for passive immunity
Environmental hygiene: clean, dry farrowing facilities
vaccineRecommendations: Autogenous S. hyicus bacterin (for use in sows in endemic herds)

diseases.name: Glässer disease (Glaesserella parasuis)
diseases.description: Glässer's disease caused by Glaesserella parasuis (formerly Haemophilus parasuis) is characterized by fibrinous polyserositis, meningitis, and arthritis in nursery pigs 4–8 weeks of age. At least 15 serovars exist with variable virulence; serovars 1, 5, 10, 12, 13, 14 most pathogenic. G. parasuis is a commensal of the upper respiratory tract; disease occurs in pigs without specific immunity (SPF pigs, caesarean-derived pigs, naive herds). PRRS and other immunosuppressive viruses predispose.
clinicalSigns: Acute febrile illness: high fever (40.5–42°C), lethargy, anorexia
Painful swollen joints: carpal, tarsal, stifle joints — lameness, reluctance to move
Meningitis: paddling, opisthotonus, convulsions, lateral recumbency
Fibrinous pleuritis and pericarditis: labored breathing, coughing
Fibrinous peritonitis: abdominal pain, reluctance to walk
Classic 'Glässer's triad': polyserositis + arthritis + meningitis
Sudden death in peracute cases (septicemia)
Affected pigs typically 4–8 weeks of age (nursery stage)
transmission: Commensal of upper respiratory tract in carrier pigs
Sow-to-piglet transmission in early life provides initial exposure
Direct contact and respiratory droplets
Stress (weaning, mixing, transport) allows opportunistic invasion
Concurrent PRRSV, SIV, or PCV2 infection significantly predisposes
SPF and caesarean-derived pigs at highest risk due to absence of maternal immunity
diagnosis: Clinical: fever + arthritis + meningitis + polyserositis in nursery pigs
Post-mortem: fibrinous exudate on pleura, pericardium, peritoneum — pathognomonic
Bacterial culture: highly fastidious organism; requires enriched media with NAD + serum
PCR for G. parasuis from CSF, joint fluid, serosae
Serotyping important for vaccine matching
Differentiate from Streptococcus suis (similar presentation)
treatment: Early penicillin G treatment highly effective: 20,000 IU/kg twice daily for 3–5 days
Ceftiofur, ampicillin, or trimethoprim-sulfa as alternatives
Anti-inflammatory (NSAIDs): meloxicam, ketoprofen for pain and fever management
Parenteral route essential in acute cases
Mass treatment of entire affected group at first sign of disease
control: Vaccination: commercially available serovar-specific inactivated vaccines
Match vaccine serovars to farm isolates — serotyping important
Sow vaccination before farrowing to boost colostral antibody levels
Piglet vaccination at 3–4 weeks and 6–8 weeks
Reduce concurrent viral infections (PRRS, SIV, PCV2) to limit predisposing immunosuppression
Avoid mixing pigs from different health status sources
vaccineRecommendations: Inactivated G. parasuis bacterin (serovar-specific)
Sow vaccination 3–4 weeks pre-farrowing for colostral protection

diseases.name: Ileitis (Lawsonia intracellularis)
diseases.description: Proliferative enteropathy (PE) caused by Lawsonia intracellularis, an obligate intracellular bacterium infecting intestinal crypt epithelial cells. Presents as two main forms: chronic porcine proliferative enteropathy (PPE) in growers/finishers, and acute proliferative hemorrhagic enteropathy (PHE) in young adults and gilts. PPE causes reduced growth; PHE (bloody gut) causes acute death from intestinal hemorrhage. Widespread globally; major economic impact.
clinicalSigns: PPE (chronic): loose, watery feces ('pipe-stem' or 'hosepipe' diarrhea), non-bloody
Reduced growth rate, poor feed conversion, variable fecal consistency in groups
Pale pigmentation in chronic cases; mild to moderate anemia
PHE (acute): sudden death in gilts and young adults (15–20 weeks and older)
PHE: tarry, bloody, or black hemorrhagic diarrhea before death
PHE: pale skin, rapidly progressing weakness and collapse
Mixed group presentation: some acute deaths with others showing chronic wasting
transmission: Fecal-oral transmission: ingestion of L. intracellularis shed in feces
Organism shed in feces for extended periods by infected pigs
Environmental contamination: organism survives weeks in moist conditions
Introduced by carrier pigs — common on farms
Wild rodents as potential reservoir
diagnosis: PPE: clinical signs of loose feces + poor growth in 6–20 week old pigs
PHE: sudden death + bloody/tarry feces in young adults; post-mortem definitive
Post-mortem PHE: corrugated ('garden hose') intestinal wall, bloody content in ileum
PCR on feces: most practical diagnostic test
Serology: ELISA, IFAT for antibody detection in herd surveillance
Histopathology: intracellular curved organisms in proliferating crypt epithelial cells
treatment: Tiamulin (most effective): 10–12.5 mg/kg/day in feed or water for 10–14 days
Tylosin, lincomycin as alternatives for less severe cases
Chlortetracycline for mass medication in outbreaks
PHE (acute): individual parenteral treatment with tiamulin + supportive care
Blood transfusion in severe PHE cases where feasible
control: Vaccination: oral live attenuated L. intracellularis vaccine highly effective
Vaccination at 3–4 weeks of age; administer before field exposure
Strategic in-feed medication during high-risk period (transition to grow-finish)
All-in/all-out management in grow-finish to disrupt transmission cycles
Rodent control — reduce environmental contamination
vaccineRecommendations: Oral live attenuated Lawsonia intracellularis vaccine (e.g., Enterisol Ileitis)
Single oral dose at 3–4 weeks of age

diseases.name: Leptospirosis
diseases.description: Leptospirosis in pigs caused primarily by Leptospira pomona, L. bratislava, L. canicola, L. icterohaemorrhagiae, and L. tarassovi. Clinical manifestations depend on serovar and age: reproductive failure (abortion, stillbirths) in sows; subclinical infection in growers. L. bratislava has particular affinity for the genital tract. Zoonotic risk is significant for farm workers.
clinicalSigns: Abortion in mid-to-late gestation (most common clinical sign in sows)
Stillbirths and birth of weak, leptospiraemic piglets
Infertility: increased returns to service in affected sows
Agalactia: reduced milk production in affected sows
Acute febrile illness with icterus and hemoglobinuria in young piglets (rare)
Meningitis in neonatal piglets with L. pomona infection
Subclinical renal and hepatic infection in growing pigs
Kidney lesions ('white spots') found at slaughter — common finding
transmission: Urine of infected pigs — primary environmental contamination route
Direct contact with infected urine, placental fluids, aborted material
Venereal transmission: boar semen is an important route for L. bratislava
Contaminated water sources — organism survives weeks in moist alkaline environments
Wild animals (wildlife, rodents) as maintenance hosts and sources of introduction
ZOONOTIC: Direct contact with infected urine during farrowing is high risk for workers
diagnosis: Serology: MAT (Microscopic Agglutination Test) — gold standard; identify serovars
PCR on urine, blood, placenta for direct detection
Dark-field microscopy of urine: motile spirochetes (not practical for routine use)
Post-mortem: interstitial nephritis, 'white spots' in kidneys at slaughter
Culture: specialized EMJH medium under microaerophilic conditions — slow (weeks)
treatment: Streptomycin (25 mg/kg IM) — eliminates renal carrier state most effectively
Oxytetracycline (800g/ton feed) for 3 weeks for herd treatment
Amoxicillin or penicillin for acute clinical cases
Treat entire breeding herd during outbreak period
control: Vaccination: polyvalent leptospiral bacterin (covers major serovars incl. pomona, bratislava)
Sow vaccination 2x/year; boar vaccination every 6 months
Rodent control — major reservoir for maintenance hosts
Control standing water; improve drainage and hygiene
Test and vaccinate replacement stock before introduction
Personal protective equipment for farm workers when handling reproductive material
vaccineRecommendations: Polyvalent leptospiral bacterin (pomona, bratislava, canicola, icterohaemorrhagiae, grippotyphosa)

diseases.name: Listeriosis
diseases.description: Listeria monocytogenes causes sporadic septicemia and encephalitis in pigs, though pigs are generally less susceptible than ruminants. Infection is usually subclinical; clinical disease associated with immunosuppression or high-dose exposure. Zoonotic pathogen of major public health concern — pigs as source of contamination in food products. Ubiquitous environmental organism, particularly in silage and organic matter.
clinicalSigns: Usually subclinical in immunocompetent adult pigs
Septicemia in young piglets: fever, depression, sudden death
Encephalitis (rare): incoordination, head tilt, circling, nystagmus
Abortion and reproductive failure in sows (rare compared to ruminants)
Poor growth and general unthriftiness in affected animals
transmission: Ingestion of contaminated feed (silage most important source)
Environmental contamination: soil, decaying organic matter
Fecal-oral route between pigs
Fecal shedding of L. monocytogenes by carrier pigs
ZOONOTIC: Contaminated pork products implicated in human listeriosis outbreaks
diagnosis: Culture from blood, cerebrospinal fluid, brain, lymph nodes
Characteristic tumbling motility at room temperature on semi-solid agar
PCR for rapid identification
Histopathology: microabscesses in liver, brain in acute cases
Usually incidental finding at slaughter inspection
treatment: Ampicillin or penicillin G — drug of choice
Trimethoprim-sulfa as alternative
Treatment response variable in encephalitic form
control: Avoid feeding spoiled or poor quality silage
Maintain feed hygiene and storage quality
General biosecurity — prevent fecal contamination of feed
No vaccines available for swine

diseases.name: Mycoplasma arthritis
diseases.description: Mycoplasma hyosynoviae and Mycoplasma hyorhinis cause arthritis and polyserositis in pigs. M. hyosynoviae causes acute non-suppurative arthritis in growing-finishing pigs 10–24 weeks; predominantly affects joints. M. hyorhinis causes polyserositis, polyarthritis, and sometimes otitis media in nursery pigs 3–10 weeks. Both organisms are commensals of the respiratory tract that become opportunistic pathogens under stress.
clinicalSigns: M. hyosynoviae: acute onset lameness in one or multiple joints (10–24 weeks)
Joint distension with synovial fluid accumulation (non-suppurative)
Pain on joint manipulation; reluctance to bear weight
Normal or mildly elevated temperature
M. hyorhinis: polyserositis in nursery pigs (3–10 weeks) — similar to Glässer's disease
Fibrinous pericarditis, pleuritis, peritonitis with M. hyorhinis
Otitis media: head tilt, ear scratching, decreased hearing with M. hyorhinis
Mild respiratory signs in some M. hyorhinis cases
transmission: Carrier animals: commensal in upper respiratory tract and tonsils
Sow-to-piglet transmission in early life
Direct contact and respiratory droplets
Stress, overcrowding, and concurrent infections trigger clinical disease
diagnosis: Clinical: non-suppurative arthritis in growing pigs without fibrinous polyserositis (M. hyosynoviae)
Joint fluid: clear, slightly turbid (not purulent), high cell count — lymphocytes
PCR from joint fluid or synovial membrane — most reliable
Culture: slow-growing on specialized Hayflick medium; requires 2–4 weeks
Differentiate from Glässer's disease (G. parasuis), Streptococcal arthritis, erysipelas
treatment: Tiamulin: most effective for both M. hyosynoviae and M. hyorhinis
Lincomycin, doxycycline as alternatives
NSAIDs for pain management
Individual parenteral treatment for severe cases
control: Reduce stress factors associated with disease outbreaks
Strategic tiamulin medication during high-risk periods
No specific vaccines available

diseases.name: Pasteurellosis (Pasteurella multocida)
diseases.description: Pasteurella multocida causes progressive atrophic rhinitis (in combination with Bordetella bronchiseptica), pneumonia as part of porcine respiratory disease complex (PRDC), and occasionally septicemia. Toxigenic type D strains produce dermonecrotic toxin (DNT) causing turbinate atrophy in progressive AR. Non-toxigenic strains (types A, D) are secondary pneumonia pathogens.
clinicalSigns: Progressive AR: irreversible turbinate atrophy, snout deviation, epistaxis
Bronchopneumonia: wet productive cough, fever (40–41°C), labored breathing
Severe pneumonia: fibrinous bronchopneumonia in ventral lung lobes
Bloody nasal discharge in severe pneumonia
Septicemia (rare): sudden death, hemorrhages, septic shock
Secondary to PRRS, SIV, M. hyopneumoniae — dramatically increases severity
transmission: Commensal of upper respiratory tract of healthy pigs
Aerosol and direct contact transmission
Introduced via carrier pigs or birds in environment
Becomes pathogenic as secondary invader following viral respiratory infections
Toxigenic type D strains transmitted directly between pigs
diagnosis: Culture from nasal swabs, lung tissue, or transtracheal wash
Blood agar: characteristic mucoid colonies (capsular type A) or small convex colonies (type D)
Typing: capsule typing (A, B, D, E, F) + DNT detection for AR strains
PCR for toxin gene (toxA)
Pathology: fibrinous bronchopneumonia with ventral consolidation
treatment: Tetracyclines (in-feed or injectable)
Penicillin, ampicillin for acute cases
Trimethoprim-sulfa, ceftiofur
Treatment of primary viral infection to reduce secondary bacterial severity
control: Vaccination with P. multocida toxoid (for AR prevention) in combination with B. bronchiseptica bacterin
Vaccination of sows pre-farrowing for passive immunity
Reduce viral predisposing factors (PRRS vaccination, SIV vaccination)
Improve ventilation and stocking density
vaccineRecommendations: P. multocida toxoid + B. bronchiseptica bacterin combination vaccine

diseases.name: Postpartum Agalactia Syndrome (MMA)
diseases.description: Mastitis-Metritis-Agalactia (MMA) syndrome, also called postpartum dysgalactia syndrome (PDS), is a multifactorial disease of sows occurring within 1–3 days of farrowing. Characterized by reduced or absent milk production, mammary gland inflammation, and uterine infection. Major cause of neonatal mortality from starvation and hypoglycemia. Predisposing factors include constipation, high BCS, stress, and gram-negative bacteria (E. coli, Klebsiella).
clinicalSigns: Agalactia: sow lies away from piglets; piglets gaunt, constantly nursing, crying
Fever (39.5–40.5°C) within 12–36 hours post-farrowing
Reduced feed and water intake in affected sow
Mammary glands: normal to mildly firm or swollen (mastitis component)
Vulvar discharge: purulent or bloody uterine discharge
Depression and general malaise in affected sow
Piglet mortality from starvation, hypoglycemia, and crushing
transmission: Multifactorial: not directly transmitted
E. coli, Klebsiella from environment colonize mammary gland and uterus
Predisposing factors: constipation pre-farrowing, high feed intake, stress, poor hygiene
Sow BCS: overconditioned sows at higher risk
diagnosis: Clinical signs: agalactic sow 12–48 hours post-farrowing with failing piglets
Milk strip test: no milk or scant milk production
Rectal temperature: fever distinguishes from agalactia without fever
Milk culture: E. coli, Klebsiella, Staphylococcus from infected glands
Serum acute phase proteins (CRP, haptoglobin) elevated
treatment: Oxytocin: 10–20 IU IM or IV — promotes milk letdown in early cases
Broad-spectrum antibiotics: amoxicillin, trimethoprim-sulfa, ceftiofur
NSAIDs: flunixin meglumine, meloxicam — reduce inflammation and fever
Supplemental feeding of piglets: milk replacer while sow recovers
Laxatives (magnesium sulphate) and increased water access pre-farrowing
control: Manage constipation pre-farrowing: add bran/laxatives to sow diet in last week
Adequate water access throughout gestation (minimum 10-15 L/day at farrowing)
Avoid overconditioning of sows (target BCS 3–3.5 at farrowing)
Improve farrowing house hygiene and sanitation
Reduce stress: gradual adaptation to farrowing crate, minimize disturbances
vaccineRecommendations: E. coli vaccines may reduce MMA incidence in some herds

diseases.name: Salmonellosis
diseases.description: Salmonellosis in pigs caused primarily by Salmonella Choleraesuis (septicemia/systemic) and Salmonella Typhimurium (enteritis/colitis). S. Choleraesuis causes high-mortality septicemia with lung and liver involvement; primarily affects 8–20 week old pigs. S. Typhimurium causes mucohemorrhagic colitis with lower mortality. Major food safety and zoonotic concern — pigs are important reservoir for human Salmonella infections.
clinicalSigns: S. Choleraesuis (septicemia): high fever (41–42°C), severe depression, cyanosis of ears/extremities
Labored breathing, pneumonia signs with S. Choleraesuis
Yellowish diarrhea progressing to bloody diarrhea in septicemic cases
Skin cyanosis: purple-blue discoloration of extremities — classic sign
S. Typhimurium (enteric): profuse mucoid to bloody yellow diarrhea
Tenesmus and straining with fibrinonecrotic colitis
Weight loss, dehydration, poor growth in enteric salmonellosis
High mortality (10–20%) in septicemic S. Choleraesuis outbreaks
transmission: Fecal-oral transmission: ingestion of contaminated feed or water
Carrier pigs: shed Salmonella intermittently without clinical signs
Environmental contamination: organic matter in pens, feeders, drinkers
Introduced via contaminated feed ingredients (bone meal, meat meal)
Rodents, birds as environmental reservoirs and vectors
ZOONOTIC: Contaminated pork is major source of human Salmonella infections globally
diagnosis: Clinical: fever + cyanosis + pneumonia in 8–20 week pigs (S. Choleraesuis)
Fecal culture: selective enrichment + plating on XLD, HE, brilliant green agar
Post-mortem S. Choleraesuis: button ulcers in colon, mottled lung, necrotic foci in liver/spleen
Post-mortem S. Typhimurium: fibrinonecrotic colitis in spiral colon
Serotyping by Kauffmann-White scheme at reference laboratory
Food safety: longitudinal monitoring programs using meat juice ELISA at slaughter
treatment: Fluoroquinolones (enrofloxacin) for systemic S. Choleraesuis — most effective
Trimethoprim-sulfa, amoxicillin for enteric forms
Supportive: fluids, electrolytes for dehydrated pigs
Antibiotic resistance increasingly common — always perform sensitivity testing
Avoid unnecessary treatment of subclinical carriers — may select resistant strains
control: All-in/all-out management — critical for reducing environmental load
Rodent and bird control programs
Clean and disinfect between batches; organic acid disinfectants most effective
Competitive exclusion products for post-weaning pigs
Vaccination (S. Typhimurium live attenuated vaccine) in high-risk herds
Reduce transport stress to minimize shedding at slaughter
vaccineRecommendations: Inactivated Salmonella Typhimurium bacterin (for sow vaccination)
Live attenuated S. Typhimurium oral vaccine for growing pigs

diseases.name: Streptococcal infections
diseases.description: Streptococcus suis is the most important streptococcal pathogen of swine worldwide, causing meningitis, septicemia, arthritis, pneumonia, and endocarditis. At least 35 serotypes identified; serotype 2 most virulent and important globally. S. suis type 2 is also a significant zoonotic pathogen causing meningitis and septicemia in humans (especially in Southeast Asia). Ubiquitous commensal of tonsils and nasal cavity in carrier pigs.
clinicalSigns: Meningitis: sudden onset fever, loss of balance, recumbency, paddling, opisthotonus
Convulsions and death within hours in peracute meningitis
Septicemia: sudden death, high fever, petechiae on skin
Arthritis: hot, swollen painful joints — unilateral or bilateral lameness
Endocarditis: sudden death in finishers (audible heart murmur)
Pneumonia: coughing, labored breathing (less common than meningitis)
Primarily affects piglets 4–8 weeks and growing pigs post-weaning
transmission: Commensal in tonsils/nasal cavities of healthy carrier pigs (up to 100% of herds)
Transmission: direct contact, respiratory droplets
Sow-to-piglet transmission at farrowing
Stress (weaning, mixing, transport) triggers invasion from commensal to pathogenic state
Concurrent PRRSV and PCV2 infections significantly predispose
ZOONOTIC: Human exposure via slaughterhouse work, pig farming, wound contamination
diagnosis: Clinical: sudden meningitis/seizures/death in nursery pigs
CSF: turbid, pleocytosis with neutrophils; gram-positive cocci in chains
Blood/CSF culture: alpha or beta hemolytic streptococci; Gram-positive cocci
PCR for S. suis species confirmation and serotyping
Differentiate from Glässer's disease (G. parasuis), E. coli septicemia
Post-mortem: fibrinous meningitis, polyserositis, vegetative endocarditis
treatment: Penicillin G: treatment of choice — highly effective if administered early
High doses essential for CNS penetration (20,000 IU/kg twice daily minimum)
Ampicillin, amoxicillin as alternatives
Ceftiofur for cases with penicillin resistance
Early mass treatment of entire affected cohort critical
control: Vaccination: autogenous bacterins in problem herds (limited commercial vaccines)
Commercially available S. suis type 2 vaccines in some markets
Reduce predisposing factors: PRRS/PCV2 vaccination, reduce mixing stress
Strategic penicillin medication in feed post-weaning in high-risk herds
Minimize crowding and maintain all-in/all-out systems
vaccineRecommendations: Autogenous S. suis bacterin (farm-specific serotype)
Commercial S. suis type 2 inactivated vaccine (limited availability by region)

diseases.name: Swine dysentery (Brachyspira hyodysenteriae)
diseases.description: Swine dysentery caused by Brachyspira hyodysenteriae (and B. hampsonii, B. suanatina) is a severe ulcerative hemorrhagic fibrinous colitis primarily affecting growing-finishing pigs. Characterized by mucohemorrhagic diarrhea with blood and mucus (fibrinous) in feces. Economic impact from mortality, reduced growth, and treatment costs is high. Anaerobic organism survives in environment for weeks. Strict biosecurity essential for control.
clinicalSigns: Mucoid to mucohaemorrhagic diarrhea: feces containing blood, mucus, and fibrinous material
Straining and tenesmus: frequent, incomplete defecation attempts
High fever (40–41°C) in acute phase
Rapid weight loss, dehydration, and sunken flanks
Pale mucous membranes due to blood loss anemia
Affected pigs show hunched back, reluctance to move
High morbidity (50–90%) with mortality 1–30% in untreated outbreaks
Primarily grows/finishers 7–12 weeks of age but any age can be affected
transmission: Fecal-oral transmission via contaminated environment
Organism survives weeks in moist feces and mud at cooler temperatures
Introduced via carrier pigs — primary route for new herd infection
Birds and rodents can carry organism between premises
Contaminated boots, equipment, and vehicles spread between farms
Recovered carrier pigs shed organism intermittently for months
diagnosis: Clinical: mucohemorrhagic diarrhea + blood in feces + high morbidity in grow-finish
Dark-field microscopy of fresh feces: strongly hemolytic large spirochetes (5–6 coils)
Anaerobic culture on selective agar (CVS or Trypticase soy blood agar) at 42°C
PCR: most sensitive and specific — species/strain differentiation
Post-mortem: ulcerative fibrinous typhlocolitis — spiral colon + cecum most affected
treatment: Tiamulin (first choice): 3.5 mg/kg body weight in drinking water or feed for 5–10 days
Valnemulin as alternative pleuromutilin
Lincomycin in feed or water
Dimetridazole and metronidazole effective but restricted in food-producing animals
Increasing antimicrobial resistance — test sensitivity before treatment
control: Strict biosecurity: test all incoming pigs before introduction
Clean boots and vehicles: disinfect between farms
Rodent and bird control programs on farms
Total depopulation, disinfection, and restocking with negative pigs for eradication
All-in/all-out with thorough cleaning and disinfection of pens
No effective commercial vaccines available

diseases.name: Tetanus (Clostridium tetani)
diseases.description: Tetanus caused by Clostridium tetani toxin (tetanospasmin) affecting the nervous system, causing spastic paralysis. Entry via wounds (castration, tail docking, punctures). Uncommon in pigs but can occur where contaminated soil is present. Incubation 1–3 weeks. Tetanospasmin blocks inhibitory neurotransmitter release causing hyperexcitability.
clinicalSigns: Progressive muscle stiffness and spasm — characteristic 'saw horse' stance
Inability to open mouth (trismus/lockjaw) — masticatory muscle rigidity
Prolapse of third eyelid (nictitating membrane)
Generalized hypersensitivity to sound, touch, light
Opisthotonus: rigid backward arching of neck and spine
Convulsions triggered by stimulation
Respiratory failure due to diaphragm and intercostal muscle spasm
transmission: Spores in soil, feces, and environment enter via wounds
Castration, tail docking, teeth clipping, injection sites are common entry points
Anaerobic conditions in deep or necrotic wounds favor germination and toxin production
No pig-to-pig transmission
diagnosis: Clinical: progressive spastic rigidity + wound history
C. tetani culture from wound material under strict anaerobic conditions
PCR for toxin gene
Differentiate from strychnine poisoning, other causes of rigidity
treatment: Antitoxin (equine tetanus antitoxin): early administration neutralizes unbound toxin
Penicillin G at high doses to eliminate toxin source
Muscle relaxants: diazepam, methocarbamol for spasm control
Supportive care: dark quiet environment, minimal stimulation, IV fluids
Prognosis poor in advanced cases
control: Proper wound hygiene during routine procedures (castration, tail docking)
Use clean instruments and disinfect wounds post-procedure
Vaccination in endemic areas with tetanus toxoid
Minimize wounds and trauma during management
vaccineRecommendations: Tetanus toxoid (combined Clostridial bacterin-toxoid) in endemic areas

diseases.name: Tuberculosis (Mycobacterium avium complex)

symptomsEnhanced.name: Slaughter condemnation for lymph node lesions

symptoms: Slaughter condemnation for lymph node lesions
diseases.description: Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC) — primarily M. avium subsp. hominissuis — causes tuberculosis (TB) in pigs. Commonly found as incidental lesion at slaughter in submandibular and mesenteric lymph nodes (lymphadenitis). Infection via ingestion of contaminated feed or litter from poultry. True generalized TB lesions less common. Zoonotic significance: M. avium can cause disease in immunocompromised humans. Leads to significant condemnation losses at slaughter.
clinicalSigns: Usually subclinical — discovered at slaughter inspection
Submandibular lymph node enlargement (detectable in severe cases)
Mesenteric lymph node enlargement with caseous necrosis
Generalized disease (rare): progressive weight loss, weakness
Lymphadenopathy detectable on farm in severe cases
No specific clinical signs in most field cases
transmission: Ingestion of poultry litter, soil, or water contaminated with M. avium
Shared housing or bedding with infected poultry — major risk factor
Mycobacteria highly persistent in environment (years in soil)
Infected feeds (sawdust bedding from contaminated sources)
diagnosis: Usually discovered at ante-mortem/post-mortem inspection at slaughter
Caseous or calcified granulomatous lesions in lymph nodes
Ziehl-Neelsen acid-fast staining of tissue sections
Culture on Löwenstein-Jensen medium (weeks to grow)
PCR for MAC species identification
Tuberculin skin test: limited utility in pigs
treatment: No treatment warranted in food-producing pigs
Long antibiotic courses required but not practical or approved
control: Separate pig housing from poultry — prevent shared environment
Do not use poultry litter as bedding for pigs
Purchase pigs from clean herds; investigate high condemnation rates at slaughter
Herd testing and monitoring when condemnation rates are high

diseases.name: Yersinia infection
diseases.description: Yersinia enterocolitica and Y. pseudotuberculosis cause enterocolitis in pigs, though infection is most commonly subclinical. Pigs are the primary reservoir for Y. enterocolitica biovar 4, serotype O:3 — the most common Yersinia serovar causing human foodborne illness in temperate regions. Infection occurs in growing-finishing pigs via fecal-oral route. Clinical disease rare in pigs but important for food safety.
clinicalSigns: Subclinical in most pigs — primary reservoir without disease
Mild diarrhea in weanlings (rarely significant)
Y. pseudotuberculosis: diarrhea, wasting, and death in some cases
Mesenteric lymphadenitis at slaughter — incidental finding
No specific clinical signs useful for farm-level diagnosis
transmission: Fecal-oral route between pigs
Contaminated water and feed
Environment: survives in cold, moist conditions (psychrotrophic organism — grows at 4°C)
ZOONOTIC: Contaminated pork products, especially pork neck meat
Human infections from handling raw pork or eating undercooked products
diagnosis: Isolation from feces or mesenteric lymph nodes on CIN agar (cold enrichment)
PCR for virulence genes (yadA, inv) for pathogenic strains
Usually not diagnosed clinically on farm — found at slaughter inspection
Serology (O:3 agglutination) for seroprevalence surveys
treatment: Treatment not warranted for subclinical carrier pigs
If needed: fluoroquinolones, trimethoprim-sulfa
Food safety measures more important than clinical treatment
control: Reduce fecal contamination of carcasses at slaughter
Decontamination of tonsils at slaughter — major source of carcass contamination
Pre-harvest management: reduce carrier status before slaughter
Hygiene of slaughter facilities and cold chain management

diseases.name: African Swine Fever (ASF)
diseases.description: African Swine Fever (ASF) is caused by a large DNA virus (Asfivirus), the only member of the family Asfarviridae. ASF is a highly contagious, often fatal hemorrhagic viral disease of domestic pigs and wild boars. No effective vaccine or treatment available. Multiple forms: peracute (sudden death), acute (fever, hemorrhage, 100% mortality), subacute, and chronic. Currently spreading globally (Eastern Europe, Asia as of 2018). NOTIFIABLE disease worldwide.
clinicalSigns: Peracute: sudden death without premonitory signs; high body temperature just before death
Acute: high fever (40.5–42°C), extreme depression, anorexia within 2–4 days
Cyanosis/hemorrhages: blotchy purple-red discoloration on ears, snout, abdomen
Bloody diarrhea and vomiting in some cases
Respiratory distress, labored breathing
Conjunctivitis and ocular discharge
Abortion at any stage in affected sows
Near 100% case fatality rate in acute form with highly virulent strains
Chronic form: intermittent fever, stunted growth, skin lesions, pneumonia
transmission: Direct contact with infected pigs (blood, secretions, excretions most infective)
Feeding raw/improperly processed garbage containing infected pork
Soft ticks (Ornithodoros species) — biological vector in Africa and Sardinia
Contaminated fomites: equipment, vehicles, clothing, feed
Environmental persistence: infectious in frozen meat for years
Movement of live pigs or infected pork products — primary spread mechanism
diagnosis: NOTIFIABLE: Immediately report to veterinary authorities
Real-time PCR: detection of ASFV genome — most sensitive and rapid
Antigen ELISA for virus detection in blood and tissues
Virus isolation in porcine macrophages or porcine alveolar macrophages (PAM)
ELISA, IPMA, IFT for antibody detection (subacute/chronic survivors)
Hemadsorption (HAd) test: characteristic aggregation of red blood cells around infected macrophages
treatment: NO effective treatment available
NO approved vaccine available
Supportive care has no impact on highly virulent strains
Immediate stamping-out and disposal of all pigs on affected premises
control: NOTIFIABLE: Immediate notification and stamping-out policy
Strict movement controls on pigs and pork products
Enhanced biosecurity: prevent access of wild boar; secure fencing
Prohibition on swill/garbage feeding
Immediate culling and safe disposal of all pigs on infected premises
Thorough disinfection with alkaline disinfectants (sodium hydroxide, glutaraldehyde)
Surveillance of wild boar populations in affected regions

diseases.name: Aujeszky's disease (Pseudorabies, PRV)
diseases.description: Aujeszky's disease (AD) caused by Suid alphaherpesvirus 1 (pseudorabies virus, PRV) affects pigs and many other species. In pigs: reproductive failure in sows, respiratory disease in growing pigs, fatal neurological disease in young piglets. Adults may show mild respiratory signs. Fatal 'mad itch' in cattle and other non-porcine species. Virus establishes latency in trigeminal ganglia. Eradicated from domestic pig population in many countries; remains in wild boar. NOTIFIABLE.
clinicalSigns: Neonates (< 2 weeks): high fever, convulsions, paddling, death within 24–72 hours (near 100% mortality)
Weaners/growing pigs: fever, respiratory signs, cough, rhinitis
Neurological signs in young pigs: circling, paddling, opisthotonus
Reproductive failure in sows: abortion, mummification, stillbirths, weak piglets
Adults: mild respiratory disease; fever, anorexia, depression
Boars: orchitis and reduced semen quality
High mortality in young piglets; very low in adult pigs
transmission: Direct contact with nasal and oral secretions of infected pigs
Aerosol transmission over several km between farms under favorable conditions
Semen from infected boars
Vertical transmission: transplacental and at farrowing
Wild boar as reservoir — major risk for domestic pig farms in infected regions
Contaminated feed (rarely)
