diagnosis: NOTIFIABLE in most countries
Clinical signs in young piglets (fatal neurological disease) highly suggestive
Virus isolation from tonsil, brain, lung on cell culture
PCR on brain, tonsil, trigeminal ganglia — most sensitive
ELISA: gB-ELISA (total antibody) and gE-ELISA (natural infection vs. vaccine marker)
Virus neutralization test for confirmation
treatment: No effective antiviral treatment available
Supportive care may extend life in valuable animals but cure is not possible
Vaccination controls disease but does not eliminate virus
control: NOTIFIABLE: Report immediately in countries with eradication programs
Modified live marker vaccines (gE-deleted) for vaccination in endemic areas
DIVA principle: gE-ELISA differentiates vaccinated (gE negative) from naturally infected (gE positive) pigs
Test-and-cull programs for eradication
Strict biosecurity: prevent contact with wild boar
Eradicated from domestic pigs in USA, most of EU — wild boar remains reservoir
vaccineRecommendations: gE-deleted marker MLV vaccine (allows DIVA testing)
Inactivated gE-deleted subunit vaccine
Sow vaccination pre-farrowing; piglet vaccination at 8 weeks

diseases.name: PRRS (Porcine Reproductive & Respiratory Syndrome)
diseases.description: Porcine Reproductive and Respiratory Syndrome (PRRS) caused by porcine arterivirus (PRRSV) types 1 and 2. PRRS is arguably the most economically devastating swine disease worldwide. Reproductive form: abortion, mummification, stillbirths, weak piglets in sows. Respiratory form: interstitial pneumonia, reduced growth, increased mortality in pigs of all ages. Predisposes to secondary bacterial infections. Virus establishes persistent infection in lymphoid tissue.
clinicalSigns: Reproductive failure: abortion at any stage, premature farrowing (usually day 100-115)
Increased mummified, stillborn, and weak piglets (PRRS piglets)
Sow fever (40.5–41.5°C), anorexia, lethargy in acute outbreak
Transient blue discoloration of ears in some sows ('blue ear disease')
Respiratory signs in suckling pigs: dyspnea, tachypnea, expiratory effort
Growing pigs: fever, respiratory distress, increased secondary bacterial pneumonia
Dramatically increased susceptibility to secondary infections (Glässer's, App, S. suis)
Chronic: reduced growth, increased medication costs, decreased herd productivity
transmission: Direct contact: primary transmission route via respiratory secretions
Semen from infected boars — important for naive herd introduction
Transplacental infection of fetuses in last trimester
Aerosol transmission: documented within farms; limited long-range spread under most conditions
Contaminated needles, equipment, and vehicles
Airborne spread demonstrated under specific weather conditions (temperature inversions)
diagnosis: RT-PCR on serum, oral fluid, semen, lung tissue — most sensitive during acute viremia
ELISA antibody detection (seroconversion from 10–14 days post-infection)
PRRS ELISA S/P ratio interpretation: > 0.4 positive
Virus isolation on MARC-145 cells or primary alveolar macrophages
Sequencing of ORF5 for strain comparison and outbreak tracking
IHC on lung tissue: PRRSV antigen in alveolar macrophages

Supportive: anti-inflammatory drugs reduce fever and respiratory distress
Antibiotics for secondary bacterial infections (ceftiofur, florfenicol)
Increase ventilation and reduce stocking density during outbreaks

Inactivated vaccines: limited efficacy against heterologous strains
PRRS stability programs: control virus within herd through strategic MLV exposure
Elimination programs: whole-herd exposure + closure + rollover into negative population
Air filtration systems proven to reduce airborne PRRSV entry
Strict biosecurity: test all incoming pigs; secure boar semen sources

Inactivated PRRS vaccine
Autogenous MLV derived from farm isolate in some programs

diseases.name: Blue eye disease
diseases.description: Blue eye disease caused by porcine rubulavirus (Blue Eye paramyxovirus, BEPV) is endemic in Mexico, causing corneal opacity ('blue eye'), reproductive failure, and neurological signs. Limited to Mexico and surrounding areas. BEPV infects pigs of all ages; clinical presentation varies: neurological disease in young pigs, reproductive failure in adults.

Neurological signs in young pigs: ataxia, convulsions, paddling
Reproductive failure in sows: abortion, increased stillbirths, return to service
Orchitis and testicular atrophy in boars — infertility
Respiratory signs: mild cough, dyspnea in some cases
High mortality in suckling piglets with neurological involvement

Aerosol transmission within herds
Geographic restriction: primarily Mexico and Central America

Virus isolation from corneal swabs, brain, tonsil

diseases.name: BVDV
diseases.description: Bovine viral diarrhea pestiviruses (BVDV-1, BVDV-2) occasionally infect pigs. Mainly reproductive problems in sows. Rare.

diseases.name: Classical Swine Fever (CSF, Hog Cholera)
diseases.description: Classical Swine Fever (CSF), also known as Hog Cholera, is caused by Classical swine fever virus (CSFV), a pestivirus. Highly contagious, notifiable disease with severe economic impact. Clinical severity depends on virus strain virulence: acute (high virulence) to chronic (low virulence). Affects all ages; wild boar reservoir in many regions. Eradicated from North America, Australia, Japan, and Western EU; endemic in parts of Eastern Europe, Asia, Latin America.

Conjunctivitis with mucopurulent ocular discharge
Constipation followed by diarrhea (often hemorrhagic)
Purple-red skin discoloration (hemorrhagic diathesis): ears, tail, limbs, abdomen
Leukopenia (decreased white blood cell count) in early disease
Neurological signs: ataxia, convulsions, circling in severe cases
Abortion, mummification, stillbirths in pregnant sows
Chronic low-virulence: intermittent fever, wasting, skin lesions
Congenital infection: cerebellar hypoplasia, tremors in piglets born to infected sows

Feeding contaminated garbage (swill) — primary introduction route
Direct contact with infected pigs (saliva, urine, feces, blood)
Airborne spread over short distances
Contaminated vehicles, equipment, clothing
Wild boar — important reservoir in endemic regions
Movement of infected pigs or pork products

Clinical: fever + hemorrhagic signs + neurological signs — highly suspicious
Antigen detection: immunofluorescence on tonsil, spleen, lymph nodes
RT-PCR on blood, tonsil, spleen — most sensitive and rapid
ELISA for CSFV antibodies (serosurveys, survivors)
Virus isolation on PK-15 cells — definitive confirmation
Post-mortem: splenic infarcts, 'button ulcers' in colon, hemorrhages in lymph nodes

Immediate stamping-out of affected and contact herds
Supportive care has no impact on disease outcome

Vaccination with C-strain or E2-subunit (DIVA) vaccine in endemic countries

diseases.name: Delta coronavirus (PDCoV)

Widespread but low impact.

diseases.name: Ebola Reston virus
diseases.description: Ebola Reston strain infects pigs (other Ebola strains do not). Usually asymptomatic. **Zoonotic but no human disease from this strain**. Philippines primarily.

Philippines primarily.

diseases.name: Encephalomyocarditis (EMCV)
diseases.description: Picornavirus. **Rats are main reservoir**. Three forms: myocarditis (sudden death), reproductive failure, subclinical (most common). Geographic variation in virulence.

diseases.name: Enteroviruses (Swine enterovirus)
diseases.description: Multiple enterovirus serotypes. Usually subclinical. Occasionally associated with reproductive failure, stillbirths. Clinical significance uncertain.

diseases.name: Foot-and-mouth disease (FMD)
diseases.description: Foot-and-Mouth Disease (FMD) caused by Aphthovirus (Picornaviridae) with 7 serotypes (O, A, C, SAT1, SAT2, SAT3, Asia1). Highly contagious vesicular disease affecting cloven-hoofed animals. Pigs serve as amplifiers — exhale 3000x more virus per day than cattle. Clinical signs: vesicles on feet, snout, tongue. Low mortality but devastating economic impact from trade restrictions. Notifiable disease.

Emergency vaccination ring programs in epidemic situations

Vaccine must match circulating serotype and subtype

diseases.name: Hemagglutinating encephalomyelitis (HEV, VWD)
diseases.description: Coronavirus. **Only affects pigs <4 weeks old**. Vomiting and wasting disease. Often self-limiting. Present in many herds subclinically.

diseases.name: Influenza (Swine influenza, SIV)
diseases.description: Swine influenza caused by Influenza A virus (IAV), subtypes H1N1, H1N2, H3N2, and reassortants. Causes acute respiratory disease with high morbidity but usually low mortality. Three major lineages in pigs: classical swine (cs), human seasonal (hs), and avian-like (av). The 2009 pandemic H1N1 originated from reassortment between human and swine influenza viruses. IAV undergoes rapid antigenic drift and shift in pig populations. ZOONOTIC potential: transmission to humans documented.

High fever (40.5–41.5°C) for 2–5 days
Severe lethargy: pigs reluctant to rise, piling, huddling
Respiratory signs: labored breathing, expiratory effort, coughing
Anorexia: near-complete feed refusal during acute phase
Rapid recovery in most cases: full recovery within 7–10 days in uncomplicated cases
Conjunctivitis and nasal discharge (serous initially)
Secondary bacterial pneumonia common: markedly increases severity and mortality
Reproductive: occasional abortions and premature farrowing in affected sows

Direct contact with infected pigs

Chronic mange: thick grey-brown hyperkeratotic skin crusts, especially on ears
Ear canal crusting — pathognomonic; severe pigs scratch head violently
Reduced growth and poor feed conversion in affected herds
Mange-free herds show improved productivity after eradication
transmission: Direct animal-to-animal contact — primary and most important route
Contaminated housing: mites survive off-host for 2–3 days maximum
Boars to sows during mating
Sow to piglets post-farrowing
Introduced via infested replacement pigs
diagnosis: Clinical: intense pruritus + ear canal crusting in multiple pigs

ELISA serology: reliable for herd-level surveillance of mange status

Treat all incoming pigs on arrival

diseases.name: Metastrongylosis (Lungworm)
diseases.description: Lungworm infection caused by Metastrongylus species (M. elongatus, M. pudendotectus, M. salmi) causes verminous bronchitis and pneumonia in outdoor or extensively managed pigs. Indirect life cycle requires earthworms as intermediate host. Clinical signs most severe in young pigs with heavy infections. Lungworms may act as vectors for swine influenza virus.

Doramectin: injectable anthelmintic
Fenbendazole: in-feed anthelmintic
Levamisole injection or in drinking water

Indoor management greatly reduces lungworm exposure
Anthelmintic treatment: regular deworming of outdoor pigs
Pasture rotation to reduce earthworm parasite burden over time

diseases.name: Red stomach worm (Hyostrongylus rubidus)
diseases.description: Gastric nematode. Causes chronic gastritis, erosions, ulcers. Poor growth. Outdoor pigs primarily. Usually part of mixed nematode infection.

In-feed or injectable

Pasture rotation.

diseases.name: Toxoplasmosis (Toxoplasma gondii)
diseases.description: Intracellular protozoan. **Zoonotic (major human concern - congenital infection, immunocompromised)**. Cats definitive host. Pigs intermediate host. Usually asymptomatic in pigs.

Tissue cysts in undercooked pork → HUMANS

diseases.name: Trichinella (Trichinellosis)

symptomsEnhanced.name: Public health: zoonotic risk from undercooked pork

symptoms: Public health: zoonotic risk from undercooked pork
diseases.description: Trichinellosis caused by Trichinella spiralis (and other species) is a zoonotic parasitic disease of major public health importance. Pigs acquire infection by ingesting Trichinella-infected meat from rodents, wild animals, or other pigs. Muscle-stage larvae encyst in skeletal muscle, causing no clinical signs in pigs. Humans infected by eating raw or undercooked pork. Trichinella testing of pork is mandatory in many countries.
clinicalSigns: Pigs: usually NO clinical signs — subclinical carrier
Very heavy infections: mild fever, muscle stiffness, periorbital edema in pigs
In humans (zoonosis): fever, myalgia, periorbital edema, eosinophilia — can be fatal
Clinical signs irrelevant for diagnosis in pigs — meat inspection is critical
transmission: Pigs ingest Trichinella-infected rodents, garbage, or carcasses
Swill/garbage feeding: primary risk in commercial settings
Cannibalism (fighting, tail biting) between pigs
ZOONOTIC: Raw or undercooked pork products are primary human exposure risk
diagnosis: Meat inspection: mandatory Trichinella testing at slaughter in many countries
Digestion method (pooled sample digestion): gold standard for meat testing
ELISA serology on pig blood at slaughter
PCR for species identification in positive samples
treatment: No treatment warranted in food pigs — food safety only
Mebendazole or albendazole theoretically effective but not practically applied
control: PROHIBIT garbage/swill feeding — single most effective control measure
Eliminate rodents from pig facilities — important reservoir
Prevent contact with wildlife carcasses
Mandatory slaughter testing in many countries (EU, US regulated)
Proper cooking/freezing of pork inactivates larvae

diseases.name: Trichuriasis (Whipworm, Trichuris suis)
diseases.description: Whipworm infection caused by Trichuris suis in the large intestine (cecum and proximal colon) of pigs. Adults embedded in mucosal crypts causing typhlocolitis. Clinical signs usually subclinical to mild but heavy infections cause hemorrhagic diarrhea similar to swine dysentery. T. suis eggs extremely environmentally resistant. Important in outdoor and poorly cleaned operations.
clinicalSigns: Usually subclinical or mild
Heavy infections: mucohemorrhagic diarrhea resembling swine dysentery
Weight loss, dehydration in severe infections
Tenesmus and rectal prolapse in heavy burdens
Reduced growth performance in subclinically infected pigs
transmission: Direct life cycle: eggs passed in feces, embryonate in environment (3–4 weeks minimum)
Ingestion of embryonated eggs from contaminated soil or bedding
Eggs extremely resistant: persist in environment for years
diagnosis: Fecal flotation: characteristic barrel-shaped eggs with bipolar plugs
McMaster for quantitative egg counts
Post-mortem: white whipworm adults in cecum and proximal colon; hemorrhagic mucosa in heavy infections
Differentiate from swine dysentery — dark-field microscopy of feces
treatment: Fenbendazole or flubendazole in feed
Ivermectin (effective against whipworm)
Oxibendazole, doramectin
control: All-in/all-out with thorough cleaning and disinfection
Concrete or slatted flooring reduces environmental egg buildup
Strategic anthelmintic treatment program
Avoid soil access in grow-finish pigs where Trichuris is prevalent

diseases.name: Biotin deficiency
diseases.description: Biotin (Vitamin B7) deficiency in pigs causes hoof and skin abnormalities. Important practical concern in sows affecting foot health, reproductive performance, and litter quality. Modern commercial diets include biotin supplementation. Biotin is essential for fatty acid synthesis, gluconeogenesis, and carboxylation reactions. Deficiency most likely when feeding raw eggs (avidin binds biotin) or diets low in biotin-rich ingredients.
clinicalSigns: Cracks and fissures in hooves — horizontal cracks at hoof wall or coronary band
Lameness and painful gait due to hoof lesions
Hairy coat, scaly dermatitis
Perioral lesions (parakeratosis around lips and snout) in severe cases
Reproductive effects in sows: reduced conception rates, smaller litters
Poor wound healing
transmission: Nutritional deficiency — not infectious
Risk factors: low-biotin diets, high-sulfur amino acid content, raw egg white in diet
diagnosis: Clinical: hoof cracks + lameness + dietary history suggesting low biotin
Response to supplementation (therapeutic trial)
Plasma biotin levels (laboratory analysis)
Rule out other causes of lameness and hoof lesions (osteochondrosis, injury, erysipelas)
treatment: Oral biotin supplementation: 200–500 μg/day for affected animals
Parenteral biotin injection for rapid response in individual cases
Improvement typically seen within 4–8 weeks
control: Supplementation of sow diets with 300–500 μg biotin/day
Routinely add biotin to grow-finish diets (100–200 μg/kg)
Avoid raw egg products in swine diets
Regular floor and footbath maintenance to prevent secondary infections in cracked hooves

diseases.name: Iron deficiency anemia
diseases.description: Iron deficiency anemia is the most common nutritional deficiency in neonatal pigs. Neonatal piglets are born with limited iron stores and rapidly deplete reserves through fast growth. Sow milk contains minimal iron. Without supplementation, clinical anemia develops by 2–3 weeks of age. Standard prevention: iron dextran injection at 2–3 days of age is mandatory in commercial production.
clinicalSigns: Pallor of mucous membranes and skin
Weakness and reduced vitality — piglets reluctant to nurse or compete for teats
Increased respiratory rate due to compensatory tachypnea
Pot belly and sparse hair coat ('thumps' — sway syndrome)
'Sudden death' in apparently healthy, fast-growing piglets at 3–4 weeks of age
Reduced weight gain from 2 weeks onward
Increased susceptibility to infectious diseases
transmission: Not infectious — nutritional deficiency
Risk: fast-growing large litters with restricted access to supplemental feed
diagnosis: PCV (packed cell volume): < 30% indicative of anemia
Hemoglobin: < 80 g/L (< 8 g/dL) indicates significant anemia
Blood smear: hypochromic microcytic anemia pattern
Serum iron: reduced; TIBC increased
Response to iron supplementation confirms diagnosis
treatment: Iron dextran injection (200 mg elemental iron): immediately for clinical cases
Oral iron supplementation as alternative or supplement
Correct concurrent deficiencies (copper, folate)
control: Standard protocol: 200 mg iron dextran IM injection at 2–3 days of age for all piglets
Second injection at 2–3 weeks if heavy suckling pigs or poor initial response
Provide iron-rich soil/peat access for piglets on outdoor systems
Iron supplementation of sow diet has minimal effect on milk iron content

diseases.name: Mulberry Heart Disease (Vitamin E/Selenium deficiency)
diseases.description: Mulberry Heart Disease (MHD) caused by Vitamin E and/or selenium deficiency results in nutritional cardiomyopathy with sudden death in fast-growing pigs. Named for the characteristic mulberry-like appearance of the heart from hemorrhage and necrosis. Hepatosis dietetica (liver necrosis) and nutritional muscular dystrophy (white muscle disease) occur concurrently. Affects rapidly growing pigs 3–16 weeks.
clinicalSigns: Sudden death of best-growing pigs without premonitory signs
Found dead in dorsal recumbency with signs of cardiac failure
Muscle weakness and staggering in pigs approaching collapse
Dyspnea due to pulmonary edema
Post-mortem: characteristic mulberry heart — extensive epicardial and myocardial hemorrhage
Hydropericardium and pleural effusion
Hepatic necrosis (hepatosis dietetica) concurrent in some cases
transmission: Non-infectious nutritional deficiency
Risk factors: diets deficient in vitamin E or selenium, or high in polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFA)
Geographic: selenium-deficient soils result in deficient grains
Poor feed storage: vitamin E oxidizes rapidly in stored fat-containing feeds
diagnosis: Sudden death of best-growing pigs + characteristic mulberry heart lesion
Post-mortem: pericardial/pleural effusion, extensive cardiac hemorrhage, hepatic necrosis
Blood/liver selenium levels: < 0.1 mg/kg (wet weight) in liver
Vitamin E levels in serum and liver
Histopathology: myocardial necrosis, hepatocellular necrosis
treatment: Vitamin E (selenium tocopherol): injectable in individual affected pigs
Sodium selenite/selenate injection: 0.06–0.1 mg Se/kg body weight
Herd supplementation via water or feed of entire group
control: Ensure adequate vitamin E (100–300 IU/kg feed) and selenium (0.1–0.3 mg/kg feed)
Check selenium and vitamin E content of all feed ingredients
Monitor feed storage: prevent oxidation of vitamin E
Regular blood monitoring in breeding herd
Parenteral supplementation of selenium and vitamin E in high-risk periods

diseases.name: Rickets/Osteoporosis (Ca/P/Vit D deficiency)
diseases.description: Inadequate calcium, phosphorus, or vitamin D causes poor bone mineralization. Rickets (growing bones), osteoporosis (adult bones). Causes fractures, lameness, poor growth.
clinicalSigns: Inadequate calcium, phosphorus, or vitamin D causes poor bone mineralization.
Rickets (growing bones), osteoporosis (adult bones).
Causes fractures, lameness, poor growth.

Wrong Ca:P ratio
Lack of sunlight (Vit D)
Phytic acid binding

Radiology: poor mineralization.
Serum alkaline phosphatase elevated.

Vitamin D supplementation.
Calcium/phosphorus supplementation.

diseases.name: Aflatoxicosis
diseases.description: Aflatoxicosis caused by aflatoxins produced by Aspergillus flavus and A. parasiticus growing in contaminated feed ingredients (maize, groundnuts, cottonseed). Pigs are highly susceptible to aflatoxins. Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) most toxic; causes liver damage, immunosuppression, and is a potent carcinogen. Chronic low-level exposure more common than acute toxicosis. Causes significant production losses worldwide.

Chronic (low dose): reduced growth rate, poor feed conversion
Liver damage: icterus, hepatomegaly, coagulopathy (prolonged bleeding)
Immunosuppression: increased susceptibility to bacterial and viral infections
Reproductive effects: reduced conception, smaller litters, weak piglets
Pale tan to yellow discoloration of fat at slaughter

Fungal growth in stored grain with > 14% moisture content and temperatures 25–40°C
Field contamination possible in warm humid climates

Clinical signs + feed history + liver pathology

Supportive care: liver protectants, vitamin supplements
Mycotoxin binders (montmorillonite clay, activated charcoal) to reduce absorption
No specific antidote

Proper grain drying (< 14% moisture) before storage
Adequate grain storage conditions: dry, cool, sealed
Mycotoxin binders as feed additives in high-risk regions
Rotate feed inventory (FIFO — first in, first out)

diseases.name: Ergotism
diseases.description: Claviceps purpurea sclerotia (ergot) in grain (rye, wheat, barley). Alkaloids cause vasoconstriction → gangrene of extremities. Rare in modern feed quality control.
clinicalSigns: Claviceps purpurea sclerotia (ergot) in grain (rye, wheat, barley).

Visual inspection of grain

diseases.name: Fumonisin toxicosis
diseases.description: **Most acutely toxic mycotoxin in swine**. Fusarium verticillioides/proliferatum in corn. Causes pulmonary edema (fluid in lungs) and sudden death. Pigs uniquely sensitive (other species less affected).

Often too late.

Proper corn storage.
Dilute contaminated corn

diseases.name: Ionophore toxicity
diseases.description: Accidental inclusion of ionophores (monensin, salinomycin, narasin) intended for poultry/cattle. Pigs highly sensitive. Causes muscle degeneration, heart damage. Rare but serious feed mill accidents.

Flush between batches

diseases.name: Salt poisoning (Sodium ion toxicosis)
diseases.description: **Water deprivation** is key (salt accumulates, then sudden water access → brain swelling). Also from: excessive salt intake, salt in feed error, brine ingestion. CNS signs characteristic.

CNS signs characteristic.
Blindness
Fits / convulsions

Excessive salt in feed (mixing error)
Brine/salt ingestion
Frozen waterers

Elevated Na in serum/CSF

diseases.name: Vomitoxin/Deoxynivalenol (DON) toxicosis
diseases.description: DON mycotoxin from Fusarium in grain (wheat, corn, barley). Causes feed refusal at high levels (vomitoxin). Reduced growth, immune suppression. Very common contamination problem.

Causes feed refusal at high levels (vomitoxin).
Reduced growth, immune suppression.
Very common contamination problem.

Common in wheat, corn

Blend contaminated grain to dilute.

diseases.name: Zearalenone toxicosis
diseases.description: Zearalenone (ZEA) is a mycoestrogen produced by Fusarium species contaminating maize and other cereals. Binds estrogen receptors causing hyperestrogenism. Prepubertal gilts are most sensitive. Causes vulvovaginitis, vaginal prolapse, infertility, and false pseudopregnancy. Major cause of reproductive problems in swine operations where Fusarium-contaminated grain is fed.

Vaginal/rectal prolapse in young gilts — welfare emergency
Mammary gland development in prepubertal gilts
Infertility: anovulation, irregular estrus cycles, failure to conceive in gilts/sows
Pseudopregnancy: continued progesterone secretion without true pregnancy
Boars: testicular atrophy, reduced libido, feminization
Abortion and resorption in early pregnancy

Fusarium mold growth in field or storage conditions

Regulatory limits: typically 0.1–0.25 mg/kg (100–250 ppb) in swine feed
Serum/urine metabolites (alpha-zearalenol) by HPLC-MS

Progesterone receptor antagonists (mifepristone) in valuable animals
Manual reduction of vaginal prolapse; surgical correction if recurrent
Mycotoxin binders in feed

Separate storage of maize to prevent cross-contamination
Dilution of contaminated batches (limited effectiveness)
Mycotoxin binding agents in feed as prophylactic measure

diseases.name: Atresia ani
diseases.description: Atresia ani is a congenital defect where the anal opening is absent or malformed. One of the most common congenital defects in pigs. Simple atresia ani involves only the anal canal; complex forms involve rectum or colon (atresia recti). Without surgical correction, affected piglets die within days from intestinal obstruction. Higher prevalence in certain lines suggests genetic predisposition.

Failure to defecate from birth
Progressive abdominal distension within 24–48 hours
Straining with no fecal production
Pain and distress in affected piglets

Monitor trichothecene mycotoxin levels in pregnant sow feed

diseases.name: Congenital Tremor
diseases.description: Trembling/shaking in newborn piglets. Type A (viral - causes include BVDV exposure in utero). Type B (genetic). Usually resolves with age. Mild cases compatible with survival.

Type A (viral - causes include BVDV exposure in utero).
Type B (genetic).
Usually resolves with age.

Trembling / shaking
transmission: Type A: viral (BVDV, others)
Type B: genetic
Myelin deficiency in CNS

Resolution over weeks.
Rule out other causes of trembling

Usually self-limiting.
Euthanize severe non-ambulatory
control: Type A: avoid viral exposure during pregnancy.
Type B: genetic selection

diseases.name: Epitheliogenesis imperfecta
diseases.description: Congenital absence of skin in patches. Piglets born with raw areas (no epithelium). Variable extent. Usually lethal due to infection, fluid loss. Genetic basis suspected.
clinicalSigns: Congenital absence of skin in patches.

Variable extent.

diseases.name: Frostbite
diseases.description: Tissue freezing in cold weather. Extremities affected (ears, tail, teats, scrotum, feet). Tissue dies and sloughs. Neonates and thin pigs most susceptible. Preventable.

Extremities affected (ears, tail, teats, scrotum, feet).
Tissue dies and sloughs.
Neonates and thin pigs most susceptible.
Preventable.

Inadequate heating
Wet conditions worsen
Neonatal piglets high risk

History of cold exposure.
Progressive tissue necrosis

Prevent secondary infection.
Necrotic tissue sloughs.
Usually cosmetic damage only

Protect piglets.
Dry bedding.
Draft control

diseases.name: Gastric ulcers
diseases.description: Gastric ulcers of the pars oesophagea (squamous region) are extremely common in finishing pigs and sows. Major welfare and economic concern. Caused by physical and dietary factors causing acid accumulation: finely ground feed, reduced roughage, stress, irregular feeding, and concurrent disease. Can result in sudden death from hemorrhage or chronic blood loss anemia. Prevalence at slaughter can exceed 30–50% in intensive systems.
clinicalSigns: Acute hemorrhagic form: sudden death from massive gastric hemorrhage
Subacute: pale mucous membranes, weakness, dark tarry feces (melena), anorexia
Chronic: intermittent anorexia, weight loss, dark feces
Grinding teeth (bruxism): sign of gastric pain

Stress: transport, mixing, overcrowding

Increase particle size of feed: switch to coarsely ground or pelleted feed

Feed forage/bedding materials to stimulate gastric buffering
Ensure consistent feeding times and adequate feeder space
Reduce stress: minimize transport, mixing, overcrowding
Monitor slaughter condemnation data for herd-level prevalence

diseases.name: Hemorrhagic Bowel Syndrome (HBS)
diseases.description: Hemorrhagic Bowel Syndrome (HBS), also called intestinal hemorrhage syndrome or bloody gut, is characterized by sudden death or acute collapse in good-condition grower/finisher pigs due to massive intestinal hemorrhage. Etiology is multifactorial but Clostridium perfringens type A (alpha-toxin producing) and possibly Lawsonia intracellularis involvement suggested. High-energy diets and restricted feeding are risk factors.
clinicalSigns: Sudden death in well-conditioned grower/finisher pigs
Acute collapse: pigs found dead or in severe shock
Massive hemorrhage in small intestine segments at necropsy
Pale, blood-filled, dilated intestinal loops
Affected pigs may have been apparently healthy hours before death
High-energy diets and rapid growth associated
transmission: Non-infectious or multifactorial — exact etiology debated
C. perfringens type A spores may be involved
Risk: high-energy diet, abrupt feed changes, stress
diagnosis: Post-mortem: dark red hemorrhagic fluid-filled loops of small intestine
No pathognomonic pre-mortem signs
Exclude other causes of sudden death: cardiac, toxicosis, erysipelas
C. perfringens culture and toxin typing from intestinal contents

control: Avoid sudden high-energy diet changes: gradual transitions
Reduce meal frequency and provide ad libitum access where possible
C. perfringens bacterin vaccination in problem herds (limited evidence)
Feed management: reduce overcrowding at feeders

diseases.name: Hernias (Umbilical, Inguinal, Scrotal)
diseases.description: Umbilical hernias (most common), inguinal hernias, and scrotal hernias are congenital or acquired defects in pigs. Umbilical hernias: weakness of umbilical ring; common in pigs, often hereditary. Scrotal hernias: inguinal ring defect allowing intestine into scrotal sac. Clinical significance depends on size and risk of strangulation. Major welfare and performance concern if hernias become large or strangulated.
clinicalSigns: Umbilical hernia: soft reducible swelling at umbilicus, range from small to very large
Scrotal hernia: intestinal content within scrotal sac; non-painful unless strangulated
Strangulated hernia: sudden pain, hemorrhagic content, firm non-reducible swelling — emergency
Inguinal hernia: intestinal protrusion through inguinal canal
Large hernias: trail on ground, abraded, infected
transmission: Congenital — genetic predisposition strongly implicated
Hereditary basis: remove affected animals and their parents from breeding
diagnosis: Visual inspection: swelling at umbilicus, groin, or scrotal area
Palpation: reducible (soft, fluid/intestinal) or irreducible (firm, strangulated)
Ultrasound for strangulated differentiation
treatment: Small uncomplicated umbilical hernias: may resolve spontaneously
Surgical correction: herniorrhaphy for moderate to large hernias
Strangulated hernias: emergency surgery or euthanasia
control: Genetic selection: remove boars producing high hernia incidence
Avoid castration injuries that may predispose to scrotal hernias
Proper umbilical cord management at birth

diseases.name: Osteochondrosis (OCD)
diseases.description: Osteochondrosis (OC) and osteochondrosis dissecans (OCD) are cartilage and bone disorders affecting growing pigs, causing lameness and leg weakness in finishing pigs and gilts/boars. Characterized by failure of endochondral ossification leading to cartilage retention and articular defects. Major welfare and economic issue — leading cause of culling in breeding animals. Multifactorial: genetics, rapid growth, nutrition, floor surface.
clinicalSigns: Lameness in growing and finishing pigs (16–26 weeks)
Stiff gait, reluctance to move, hesitation to bear weight
Joint swelling: elbow, stifle, hock most commonly affected
Severe cases: inability to rise, animal down
Leg weakness in sows and gilts: culled prematurely for locomotion problems
Painful flexion and extension of affected joints
transmission: Non-infectious — multifactorial
Genetic predisposition: high-growth-rate lines more susceptible
Risk factors: rapid growth rate, high dietary energy, slippery or hard flooring
diagnosis: Clinical: lameness in finishing pigs with joint effusion
Radiography: cartilage defects, joint mice, subchondral bone lesions
Post-mortem articular cartilage lesion scoring at slaughter
Histopathology: growth plate cartilage retention
treatment: Individual: NSAIDs for pain management
Surgical intervention: removal of joint mice in valuable breeding animals
Restricted growth rate and reduced energy density in diet
control: Genetic selection for reduced OCD prevalence
Non-slip, cushioned flooring in gilt development and finishing units
Moderate growth rate: avoid excessive high-energy diets in growing pigs
Adequate dietary calcium and phosphorus
Regular locomotion scoring in gilt selection

diseases.name: Pityriasis rosea
diseases.description: Noncontagious skin condition of unknown cause. Characteristic circular red lesions ("ringworm-like"). Self-limiting in 6-8 weeks. No treatment needed. Cosmetic issue mainly.
clinicalSigns: Noncontagious skin condition of unknown cause.
Characteristic circular red lesions ("ringworm-like").

Not infectious, not genetic

Rule out ringworm (fungal culture negative), mange
treatment: None needed (resolves spontaneously in 6-8 weeks).
Cosmetic only
control: None (self-limiting, unknown cause)

diseases.name: Periweaning Failure to Thrive Syndrome (PFTS)
diseases.description: Multifactorial condition: failure to eat/drink after weaning → progressive wasting and death. Piglets "go back." Causes: weaning stress, mycotoxins, poor water access, bullying, disease.
clinicalSigns: Multifactorial condition: failure to eat/drink after weaning → progressive wasting and death.
Piglets "go back." Causes: weaning stress, mycotoxins, poor water access, bullying, disease.
transmission: Multifactorial: weaning stress, poor water/feed access, mycotoxins, competition, diseases (subclinical infections)
diagnosis: Clinical signs: progressive wasting in recently weaned pigs.
Rule out specific diseases
treatment: Identify early.
Segregate.
Assist feeding/watering.
Tube feed if needed.
Appetite stimulants.
Correct underlying causes
control: Optimize weaning management: gradual transition, good water access, low competition, feed accessibility, minimize stress

diseases.name: Porcine Stress Syndrome (PSS, Malignant Hyperthermia)
diseases.latinName: PSS, Malignant hyperthermia, PSE, Halothane gene

symptomsEnhanced.name: Skin color changes: pale then cyanotic

symptoms: Skin color changes: pale then cyanotic
diseases.description: Porcine Stress Syndrome (PSS), also called Malignant Hyperthermia, is caused by a point mutation in the ryanodine receptor 1 gene (RYR1) affecting calcium release in skeletal muscle. Pigs homozygous for the mutation (stress susceptible, hal^n/hal^n) develop fatal hyperthermia and muscle rigidity when stressed. Associated with Pale, Soft, Exudative (PSE) pork quality defects. Halothane test used for identification. Largely eliminated from commercial herds through genetic testing.
clinicalSigns: Triggered by stress: transport, heat, excitement, exercise, halothane anesthesia
Progressive muscle rigidity and trembling
Rapid breathing and hyperthermia (body temperature > 42°C)
Skin: pale to blotchy pink-red discoloration, then cyanosis
Sudden collapse and death in severe episodes
PSE pork: pale, soft, watery pork at slaughter in heterozygous and homozygous pigs
transmission: Genetic condition — autosomal recessive RYR1 mutation (halothane gene)
Inherited from both parents (homozygous susceptible: hal^n/hal^n)
Heterozygous (hal^N/hal^n): carriers, mildly affected PSE quality
diagnosis: Halothane challenge test: exposure to 3% halothane — susceptible pigs develop rigidity
DNA PCR testing for RYR1 mutation: definitive and most practical
Clinical: stress-induced hyperthermia and muscle rigidity
treatment: Remove from stressor immediately
Cold water immersion and fanning for cooling
Dantrolene sodium (ryanodine receptor blocker) if available
Supportive care: IV fluids
control: DNA testing of breeding stock: select against hal^n allele
Eliminate hal^n allele from breeding population
Reduce stress during transport, loading, high temperature
Largely eliminated from major commercial lines

diseases.name: Rectal Prolapse
diseases.description: Rectal prolapse is the protrusion of rectal mucosa or full-thickness rectum through the anal opening. Common in swine; associated with increased abdominal pressure from diarrhea, mycotoxins (zearalenone), respiratory disease (coughing), constipation, and overcrowding. Major welfare concern. Higher prevalence in grow-finish pigs, particularly in groups with high respiratory disease or coughing.
clinicalSigns: Protrusion of pink-red rectal tissue through anus
Swollen, edematous, sometimes traumatized or necrotic rectal tissue
Tenesmus (straining) perpetuating and worsening prolapse
Cannibalism by pen mates leading to rapid tissue destruction
Fresh prolapse: reducible, viable tissue
Chronic: necrotic, irreducible, septic prolapse
transmission: Not infectious — management/environmental condition
Risk factors: diarrhea, coughing, zearalenone, overcrowding, constipation
Trigger: any condition causing straining or increased abdominal pressure
diagnosis: Visual: obvious rectal protrusion
Assess viability: fresh/viable vs. necrotic/irreducible
Investigate trigger: check feed for zearalenone, assess respiratory/enteric disease in pen
treatment: Fresh, viable prolapse: manual reduction under epidural anesthesia
Purse-string suture to maintain reduction for 5–7 days
Necrotic irreducible prolapse: surgical resection or euthanasia
control: Identify and correct underlying cause: diarrhea, coughing, mycotoxin
Reduce stocking density
Monitor for and separate prolapsed pigs immediately to prevent cannibalism
Check zearalenone levels if multiple cases in young gilts

diseases.name: Rectal Stricture
diseases.description: Narrowing of rectal lumen. Usually sequel to rectal prolapse repair/damage. Feces cannot pass → progressive bloating. Eventually fatal without intervention.
clinicalSigns: Narrowing of rectal lumen.
Usually sequel to rectal prolapse repair/damage.
Feces cannot pass → progressive bloating.
Eventually fatal without intervention.
transmission: Post-rectal prolapse complication
Healing with scar tissue formation
Traumatic damage
diagnosis: Clinical signs (bloating, difficult defecation), rectal palpation (narrowing)
treatment: Manual dilation (temporary).
Surgical correction not practical.
Usually cull/euthanize
control: Prevent by proper prolapse management.
Early identification

diseases.name: Shoulder Ulcers (Pressure Sores)
diseases.description: Decubital ulcers on shoulders of thin sows (low body condition). Pressure necrosis from lying on hard floors. Welfare concern. Reflects poor body condition/management.

diseases.name: Splay Leg (Myofibrillar Hypoplasia)

symptomsEnhanced.name: Hind limb(s) splayed laterally at birth

symptoms: Hind limb(s) splayed laterally at birth
diseases.description: Splay leg (myofibrillar hypoplasia) is a congenital disorder in neonatal piglets causing inability to adduct hind limbs, leaving them spread laterally. Piglets unable to stand or nurse effectively. Multifactorial: genetic predisposition, smooth flooring, viral infection (CSFV), mycotoxins during gestation, or nutritional deficiencies. High mortality from starvation and crushing unless supported early.
clinicalSigns: Both or one hind limb splayed laterally at birth — unable to stand
Affected piglets cannot adduct limbs to maintain upright posture
Unable to nurse effectively — starvation risk
High risk of being crushed by sow
Forelimb involvement less common
Recovery possible within 7–10 days with supportive taping
transmission: Congenital — genetic and environmental factors
Risk: smooth flooring, boar genetics, CSFV in utero, mycotoxins
diagnosis: Visual at birth: bilateral or unilateral hind limb splay
Histopathology: reduced myofibrils in adductor muscles
treatment: Hobbling tape: tie hind legs together at correct width to support standing
Hand-nurse: assisted nursing, supplemental milk
Tape for 5–7 days; natural recovery in mild cases
control: Rough flooring surface in farrowing: mats, grip coating
Genetic selection: reduce incidence in boar lines
Ensure adequate vitamin E and selenium in sow diet

diseases.description: UV damage to unpigmented skin. White/pink pigs most susceptible. Outdoor systems. Acute: painful erythema. Chronic: skin cancer risk. Welfare concern.

History of sun exposure.
White pigs

NSAIDs for pain.
Antibiotics if infected.

Consider pigmented breeds.
Sunscreen impractical.

diseases.name: Thrombocytopenic Purpura (Neonatal Isoerythrolysis analog)
diseases.description: Maternal anti-platelet antibodies destroy piglet platelets. Occurs 7-21 days (after colostral antibody absorption). Causes bleeding disorder. Multiple piglets in litter affected. Rare.

Prevent trauma.

diseases.name: Torsion of stomach/intestines
diseases.description: Sudden twisting of stomach or intestines → strangulation. Causes acute death. Risk factors: genetics, feeding (bloat), activity level. Most common in sows and growers. Rare but consistently present.

diseases.name: Uterine Prolapse
diseases.description: Uterine prolapse (eversion of the uterus through the vulva) is an emergency condition in sows, typically occurring in the periparturient period. Severe welfare emergency with life-threatening hemorrhage. Predisposing factors: prolonged or difficult farrowing (dystocia), hormonal imbalances, nutritional deficiencies, excessive oxytocin use, and hypocalcemia. Requires immediate veterinary attention.
clinicalSigns: Complete eversion of uterine horn(s) through vulva — unmistakable presentation
Tissue: red-to-purple fleshy tissue mass hanging from vulva
Severe pain, straining, and distress
Rapid deterioration: shock, hemorrhage, endotoxemia
Death within hours if untreated
transmission: Non-infectious — obstetrical emergency
Risk factors: hypocalcemia, prolonged farrowing, excessive straining, large litters
diagnosis: Visual: obvious uterine prolapse — no diagnostic tests needed
Assess viability of tissue for reduction vs. amputation decision
treatment: Fresh viable prolapse: epidural anesthesia, clean and lubricate tissue, manual reduction
Retain with purse-string suture after reduction
Non-reducible/necrotic: hysterectomy
Supportive care: IV fluids, antibiotics, NSAIDs, oxytocin
control: Prevent prolonged farrowing: attend farrowings in high-risk sows
Supplemental calcium before and during farrowing in at-risk sows
Proper use of oxytocin: do not over-administer
Ensure adequate body condition score at farrowing
